book.name: 1 Corinthians
book.author: Paul the Apostle
book.writtenTo: The church in Corinth, a diverse and troubled congregation

explainer.overview: First Corinthians is a letter from the apostle Paul to a church he founded in one of the Roman Empire's most cosmopolitan and morally chaotic cities. The letter is not abstract theology -- it is crisis management. The Corinthian believers were splitting into rival factions, tolerating sexual immorality, suing each other in pagan courts, getting drunk at the Lord's Supper, turning spiritual gifts into a competition, and questioning whether the dead would be raised. Paul addresses each problem head-on with a mixture of pastoral tenderness, biting sarcasm, and soaring theology. The letter contains some of the most famous passages in the Bible: the love chapter (13), the body of Christ metaphor (12), and the resurrection argument (15).
explainer.historicalContext: Corinth was a wealthy Roman colony strategically located on a narrow isthmus connecting mainland Greece to the Peloponnese. It controlled two seaports and was a major crossroads of trade, culture, and religion. The city was famous -- even by ancient standards -- for its moral permissiveness. Temples to Aphrodite, Apollo, and other deities dotted the city. Social stratification was extreme: wealthy patrons hosted house churches while slaves and freedpeople sat alongside them, creating constant tension over status and privilege. Paul had spent eighteen months founding this church (Acts 18), but after he left, problems multiplied rapidly.
explainer.whyItMatters: First Corinthians is the most practical book in the New Testament. Nearly every issue it addresses -- factionalism in the church, sexual ethics, the role of women, how to handle disagreements among believers, the abuse of spiritual gifts, the meaning of communion, the tension between personal freedom and community responsibility -- remains a live debate in churches today. Paul's insistence that the cross of Christ overturns worldly values of power and status, and his vision of the church as one body with many diverse members, continue to challenge Christian communities that tend toward homogeneity, hierarchy, or cultural accommodation.
keyThemes: Church unity versus division and factionalism
The cross as God's wisdom versus worldly wisdom
Sexual ethics and the body as a temple of the Holy Spirit
Christian freedom and its limits -- love constrains liberty
Spiritual gifts and the body of Christ metaphor
The centrality of love (agape) in all Christian practice
The resurrection of the dead as the foundation of Christian hope
The Lord's Supper and proper worship
Meat offered to idols -- conscience and community

people.name: Paul the Apostle
people.role: Author of the letter, founder of the Corinthian church
people.bio: Paul (formerly Saul of Tarsus) was a Pharisee and persecutor of Christians who experienced a dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus. He became the most influential missionary in early Christianity, establishing churches across the Roman Empire. He had spent eighteen months in Corinth founding the church there and now writes from Ephesus to address the many problems that have emerged in his absence. His tone in this letter ranges from fatherly affection to withering sarcasm.
people.culturalContext: Paul was uniquely positioned to address the Corinthian church. As a Roman citizen, trained rabbi, and Hellenistic Jew from Tarsus (a major center of Greek learning), he could navigate both Jewish and Greco-Roman cultural worlds. His tent-making trade made him financially independent, which he pointedly contrasts with other apostles who accepted support from churches.

people.name: Apollos
people.role: Eloquent teacher who ministered in Corinth after Paul
people.bio: Apollos was a Jewish believer from Alexandria, Egypt -- one of the intellectual capitals of the ancient world. He was described in Acts 18:24 as eloquent and learned, with a thorough knowledge of the Scriptures. After Paul left Corinth, Apollos came and continued teaching there. Some Corinthians began claiming allegiance to Apollos over Paul, contributing to the factionalism Paul addresses. Paul himself harbors no ill will toward Apollos and considers him a fellow worker.
people.culturalContext: Alexandria was the center of allegorical biblical interpretation and sophisticated Greek rhetoric. Apollos likely brought a more polished, intellectually appealing preaching style that some Corinthians preferred over Paul's simpler approach. The Apollos-versus-Paul rivalry reflected the Greco-Roman culture's obsession with rhetorical skill.

people.name: Cephas (Peter)
people.role: Apostle whose name was invoked by a Corinthian faction
people.bio: Cephas is the Aramaic name for Peter, the lead apostle among Jesus' original twelve disciples. Some Corinthians claimed to follow Cephas, likely emphasizing his direct connection to the historical Jesus. Whether Peter actually visited Corinth is debated, but his authority as the chief apostle made his name a powerful rallying point for those who wanted to claim the most prestigious lineage.
people.culturalContext: In a culture obsessed with patronage and social connections, claiming to follow the apostle who had walked with Jesus carried enormous social capital. The Cephas faction may have been Jewish Christians who emphasized continuity with Jerusalem and the original apostles.

people.name: Sosthenes
people.bio: Sosthenes is named as co-sender of the letter alongside Paul. He may be the same Sosthenes who was the ruler of the synagogue in Corinth and was beaten by a mob in Acts 18:17. If so, he had converted from Judaism and joined Paul's mission. His inclusion as co-author lends the letter additional authority and suggests he was well known to the Corinthian church.
people.culturalContext: Co-authorship in ancient letters was common and served to bolster the letter's authority. If this Sosthenes was indeed the former synagogue ruler, his conversion story would have been well known in Corinth.

people.name: Chloe
people.role: Informant whose household reported the Corinthian divisions
people.bio: Chloe is mentioned only in 1 Corinthians 1:11 as the head of a household whose members reported the factions in Corinth to Paul. She was likely a wealthy woman -- possibly a businesswoman with employees or slaves who traveled between Corinth and Ephesus. Paul names her openly as his source, which suggests she was a respected figure whose word carried weight.
people.culturalContext: Women heading households in the Greco-Roman world typically indicated widowhood or independent wealth. That Paul identifies her by name and treats her household's report as credible shows that women held significant roles in the early church's information networks.

places.name: Corinth
places.description: A major Roman colony in Greece, situated on the narrow isthmus connecting mainland Greece to the Peloponnese. It controlled two seaports (Lechaeum on the west and Cenchreae on the east) and was one of the wealthiest and most diverse cities in the Roman Empire. Paul founded the church here during an eighteen-month stay.
places.culturalSignificance: Corinth was destroyed by Rome in 146 BC and rebuilt by Julius Caesar as a Roman colony in 44 BC. Its population was a mix of Roman veterans, Greek locals, Jews, freedpeople, and immigrants from across the empire. The city was infamous for its moral permissiveness -- the Greek verb 'to Corinthianize' meant to practice sexual immorality. Multiple pagan temples dominated the cityscape, including a prominent temple to Aphrodite on the Acrocorinth.
places.modernLocation: Near the modern city of Corinth in southern Greece, about 80 km west of Athens.

places.name: Ephesus
places.description: A major city in the Roman province of Asia (modern Turkey) from which Paul wrote this letter. Paul spent about three years in Ephesus, making it his base for missionary work across the region.
places.culturalSignificance: Ephesus was the fourth-largest city in the Roman Empire, home to the Temple of Artemis (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World). It was a center of commerce, religion, and Roman administration. Paul's long stay there allowed him to maintain contact with his other churches, including Corinth, through messengers and letters.
places.modernLocation: Archaeological ruins near the town of Selcuk in Izmir Province, western Turkey.

timeline.event: Julius Caesar refounds Corinth as a Roman colony
Paul arrives in Corinth and spends eighteen months founding the church (Acts 18)
Paul brought before the proconsul Gallio in Corinth (Acts 18:12)
Paul writes a previous letter to Corinth (referenced in 5:9, now lost)
Chloe's household reports divisions in the Corinthian church to Paul in Ephesus
The Corinthians send Paul a letter with questions about marriage, food, and gifts
Paul writes 1 Corinthians from Ephesus, addressing both the reported problems and their questions
Paul makes a painful visit to Corinth (referenced in 2 Corinthians 2:1)
Paul visits Corinth for three months, writes Romans from there

explainer.summary: Paul opens with a warm greeting but quickly pivots to the first and most urgent problem: the Corinthian church has fractured into rival factions, each claiming loyalty to a different leader -- Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or Christ himself. Paul is appalled. He argues that the cross of Christ demolishes all human systems of status and wisdom. God deliberately chose what the world considers foolish and weak to shame the wise and powerful. The message of a crucified Messiah is absurd by every human standard -- and that is exactly the point.
themes: The cross as foolishness to the world
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter establishes the letter's central tension: the Corinthians are importing the competitive, status-obsessed values of their culture into the church. Nearly every problem Paul addresses in the rest of the letter flows from this root issue.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Summoned, invited, appointed
wordStudies.meaning: Summoned, invited, appointed. Paul uses this word three times in his opening (called apostle, called saints, called into fellowship). The Corinthians did not choose God; God chose them. This undercuts the factionalism -- you do not pick your leader; God called you.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Stupidity, absurdity
wordStudies.meaning: Stupidity, absurdity. The English word 'moron' comes from this root. Paul deliberately uses the harshest possible word for how the cross appears to the world -- not just unwise but moronic. Yet this 'moronic' message is the power of God.

verses.text: Paul, called to be an apostle of Jesus Christ through the will of God, and our brother Sosthenes,
verses.explainer: Paul identifies himself as 'called to be an apostle' -- not self-appointed but divinely commissioned. He names Sosthenes as co-sender, likely the same man who was beaten by a mob outside the Corinthian synagogue (Acts 18:17) and subsequently converted.

verses.text: to the assembly of God which is at Corinth; those who are sanctified in Christ Jesus, called to be saints, with all who call on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ in every place, both theirs and ours:
verses.explainer: Paul addresses the church 'at Corinth' but immediately expands the audience to 'all who call on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ in every place.' This is not just a private letter to one congregation -- it carries universal implications.

verses.text: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.explainer: The standard Pauline greeting blends Greek ('grace') and Hebrew ('peace') -- charis and shalom. This is not mere formality; it reflects Paul's conviction that the gospel unites the Gentile and Jewish worlds.

verses.text: I always thank my God concerning you, for the grace of God which was given you in Christ Jesus;
verses.explainer: Paul begins with thanksgiving, as was customary in ancient letters. Despite all the problems he is about to address, he starts by affirming what God has done among them. He models the grace he will later demand they show each other.

verses.text: that in everything you were enriched in him, in all speech and all knowledge;
verses.explainer: The Corinthians were 'enriched in all speech and all knowledge.' This is both a genuine compliment and a subtle setup -- they have gifts, but they are misusing them. The very gifts Paul praises here become problems later in the letter (chapters 12-14).

verses.text: even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you:
verses.explainer: The testimony of Christ was 'confirmed' in them -- their spiritual gifts are evidence that the gospel took root. Paul will not deny their giftedness; he will redirect it.

verses.text: so that you come behind in no gift; waiting for the revelation of our Lord Jesus Christ;
verses.explainer: They 'come behind in no gift.' The Corinthian church was spectacularly gifted -- tongues, prophecy, knowledge, wisdom. Their problem was never a lack of spiritual power but a lack of spiritual maturity to use it well.

thenVsNow.then: In the early church, dramatic spiritual gifts like speaking in tongues and prophecy were common and highly valued. The Corinthians treated them as markers of spiritual superiority.
thenVsNow.now: Churches today still debate the role of charismatic gifts. The Corinthian pattern -- prizing spectacular gifts over character -- remains a recurring temptation in Christian communities.

verses.text: who will also confirm you until the end, blameless in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.explainer: God 'will also confirm you until the end.' Despite the mess in Corinth, Paul expresses confidence that God will sustain them. This is not confidence in the Corinthians but confidence in God's faithfulness.

verses.text: God is faithful, through whom you were called into the fellowship of his Son, Jesus Christ, our Lord.
verses.explainer: God is faithful. This is the theological anchor for everything that follows. The Corinthians are unfaithful, confused, and divided -- but God's faithfulness does not depend on theirs. They were called into fellowship (koinonia) with Christ, and God will see it through.

verses.text: Now I beg you, brothers, through the name of our Lord, Jesus Christ, that you all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you, but that you be perfected together in the same mind and in the same judgment.
verses.explainer: Now Paul drops the hammer. 'I beg you, brothers' -- the language is urgent. He appeals for unity: same speech, no divisions, same mind and judgment. After nine verses of polite thanksgiving, the real letter begins.

thenVsNow.then: Ancient Greco-Roman society was built on patron-client relationships. People attached themselves to powerful figures for social advancement. The Corinthians were treating apostles the same way -- as celebrity patrons.
thenVsNow.now: Celebrity pastor culture in modern Christianity mirrors the Corinthian problem almost exactly. Churches that center around a personality rather than Christ tend to fracture when the personality disappoints or leaves.

verses.text: For it has been reported to me concerning you, my brothers, by those who are from Chloe’s household, that there are contentions among you.
verses.explainer: Paul names his source: Chloe's household has reported the factions. By naming her openly, Paul shows that this is not gossip or rumor but a credible report from a respected member of the community.

verses.text: Now I mean this, that each one of you says, “I follow Paul,” “I follow Apollos,” “I follow Cephas,” and, “I follow Christ.”
verses.explainer: The four slogans reveal the factions: 'I follow Paul,' 'I follow Apollos,' 'I follow Cephas,' 'I follow Christ.' Each group claims a superior allegiance. Even the 'I follow Christ' group is problematic -- they are using Jesus as a faction leader rather than as the one who unites all factions.

verses.text: Is Christ divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized into the name of Paul?
verses.explainer: Paul fires three rhetorical questions: Is Christ divided? Was Paul crucified for you? Were you baptized into Paul's name? The expected answer to all three is an emphatic no. Christ cannot be parceled out among factions.

verses.text: I thank God that I baptized none of you, except Crispus and Gaius,
verses.explainer: Paul is suddenly grateful he baptized almost nobody in Corinth. If he had, people would claim special status from their baptizer, turning a unifying sacrament into another source of division.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Baptism and patronage', 'explanation': 'In Greco-Roman culture, the person who initiated you into a group became your patron, and you owed them loyalty. Paul fears the Corinthians have turned baptism into a patronage relationship, where the baptizer becomes a faction leader.'}

verses.text: so that no one should say that I had baptized you into my own name.
verses.explainer: Paul wants no one saying they were baptized 'into his name.' Baptism is into Christ, not into any human leader. The sacrament belongs to Jesus, not to the one who administers it.

verses.text: (I also baptized the household of Stephanas; besides them, I don’t know whether I baptized any other.)
verses.explainer: A parenthetical correction -- Paul also baptized the household of Stephanas. His faulty memory adds a touch of humanity to the letter. Paul is not a polished essayist; he is dictating urgently.

verses.text: For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the Good News—not in wisdom of words, so that the cross of Christ wouldn’t be made void.
verses.explainer: Christ sent Paul to preach the gospel, not to baptize. And not with 'wisdom of words' -- clever rhetoric -- lest the cross be emptied of its power. This sets up the major argument of chapters 1-4: human eloquence and wisdom can actually undermine the gospel.

verses.text: For the word of the cross is foolishness to those who are dying, but to us who are saved it is the power of God.
verses.explainer: The word of the cross is 'foolishness' (moria) to those who are perishing but the power of God to those being saved. Paul draws a stark line: there are two ways to hear the gospel, and the difference determines everything.

thenVsNow.then: Crucifixion was the most shameful form of execution in the Roman world -- reserved for slaves, rebels, and the lowest criminals. Preaching a crucified man as Lord and Savior was, to Roman ears, genuinely absurd.
thenVsNow.now: Two thousand years of Christian culture have domesticated the cross into jewelry and church architecture. The original shock value is almost impossible to recover. Imagine telling someone that the electric chair is a symbol of hope.

verses.text:
For it is written,
“I will destroy the wisdom of the wise,
I will bring the discernment of the discerning to nothing.”
verses.explainer: Paul quotes Isaiah 29:14: God will destroy the wisdom of the wise. This is not anti-intellectual; it is anti-arrogant. The problem is not human intelligence but human confidence that intelligence alone can reach God.

verses.text: Where is the wise? Where is the scribe? Where is the lawyer of this world? Hasn’t God made foolish the wisdom of this world?
verses.explainer: Where is the wise person? The scribe? The debater? Paul surveys the intellectual landscape and declares it bankrupt. God has made the world's wisdom look foolish -- not by out-arguing it but by doing something wisdom could never have predicted: saving the world through a crucifixion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Wise, scribe, debater', 'explanation': 'Paul names the three intellectual traditions of his world: Greek philosophers (the wise), Jewish Torah scholars (scribes), and Greco-Roman rhetoricians (debaters). All three systems of thought stumble over the cross.'}

verses.text: For seeing that in the wisdom of God, the world through its wisdom didn’t know God, it was God’s good pleasure through the foolishness of the preaching to save those who believe.
verses.explainer: The world's wisdom could not find God. This is Paul's thesis: human intellectual effort, however brilliant, cannot bridge the gap between humanity and God. So God chose a different method -- the seemingly moronic message of a crucified savior -- to save those who believe.

verses.text: For Jews ask for signs, Greeks seek after wisdom,
verses.explainer: Jews demand signs (miraculous proof) and Greeks seek wisdom (philosophical sophistication). These are the two dominant approaches to truth in Paul's world, and the cross satisfies neither one.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Signs and wisdom', 'explanation': 'Jewish tradition expected the Messiah to perform dramatic miracles like Moses. Greek philosophical culture valued logical argumentation and rhetorical beauty. A crucified Messiah offered neither a power display nor an elegant argument.'}

verses.text: but we preach Christ crucified; a stumbling block to Jews, and foolishness to Greeks,
verses.explainer: Paul preaches Christ crucified -- a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles. The Greek word for stumbling block is skandalon (scandal). A crucified Messiah was not just disappointing to Jews; it was scandalous, because Deuteronomy 21:23 declared anyone hanged on a tree to be cursed by God.

verses.text: but to those who are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ is the power of God and the wisdom of God.
verses.explainer: But to those who are called -- both Jews and Greeks -- Christ is the power of God and the wisdom of God. The cross is simultaneously the answer to what Jews want (power) and what Greeks want (wisdom), but in a form neither expected.

verses.text: Because the foolishness of God is wiser than men, and the weakness of God is stronger than men.
verses.explainer: God's foolishness is wiser than human wisdom, and God's weakness is stronger than human strength. Paul's rhetoric reaches its peak. If the cross is God being 'foolish' and 'weak,' then God at his lowest still infinitely exceeds humanity at its highest.

verses.text: For you see your calling, brothers, that not many are wise according to the flesh, not many mighty, and not many noble;
verses.explainer: Paul turns from theology to sociology: look at who God called. Not many wise by human standards, not many powerful, not many of noble birth. The Corinthian church was mostly lower-class -- slaves, freedpeople, manual laborers, with a few wealthy patrons.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Social composition of the church', 'explanation': 'Archaeological and literary evidence confirms that early Christian communities were predominantly lower-class, though a few wealthy patrons provided meeting spaces and financial support. This social mix was itself countercultural in a rigidly stratified society.'}

verses.text: but God chose the foolish things of the world that he might put to shame those who are wise. God chose the weak things of the world, that he might put to shame the things that are strong;
verses.explainer: God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise. God chose the weak things to shame the strong. This is not accidental -- it is deliberate divine strategy. God's kingdom advances through the last people the world would expect.

verses.text: and God chose the lowly things of the world, and the things that are despised, and the things that are not, that he might bring to nothing the things that are:
verses.explainer: God chose the base, the despised, the things that 'are not' -- the nobodies -- to bring to nothing the things that are. In a status-obsessed city like Corinth, this was radical. The people at the bottom of the social hierarchy are God's chosen instruments.

verses.text: that no flesh should boast before God.
verses.explainer: The purpose: so that no one may boast before God. Every form of human pride -- intellectual, social, economic, religious -- is leveled at the cross. If God saves through foolishness and weakness, no one can claim credit.

thenVsNow.then: Boasting was a respected social practice in the Greco-Roman world. Public self-promotion was how you advanced in society. Paul's rejection of boasting was countercultural.
thenVsNow.now: Modern culture, especially social media, runs on self-promotion and personal branding. Paul's insistence that the gospel eliminates boasting remains as countercultural as ever.

verses.text: Because of him, you are in Christ Jesus, who was made to us wisdom from God, and righteousness and sanctification, and redemption:
verses.explainer: Christ Jesus became for us wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption. Everything the Corinthians were seeking through human effort -- wisdom, moral standing, spiritual progress, freedom -- is found in Christ, not in their own achievements.

verses.text: that, according as it is written, “He who boasts, let him boast in the Lord.”
verses.explainer: Paul quotes Jeremiah 9:24: 'Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord.' If you must boast -- and the Corinthians clearly want to -- boast about what God has done, not about your own spiritual resume.

explainer.summary: Paul reminds the Corinthians that when he first came to them, he deliberately avoided impressive rhetoric and instead preached the plain message of Christ crucified. His effectiveness came from the Spirit's power, not his eloquence. He then introduces a profound idea: God has a hidden wisdom, planned before the ages, that no worldly ruler understood -- if they had, they would never have crucified the Lord of glory. This wisdom is accessible not through human intellect but through the Holy Spirit, who searches the deep things of God.
themes: God's hidden wisdom revealed through the Spirit
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 2 deepens the argument of chapter 1. If the cross is God's true wisdom (ch. 1), then the Spirit is the means by which humans can grasp it (ch. 2). The Corinthians valued rhetorical brilliance; Paul says the Spirit's power matters infinitely more.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A secret plan now revealed
wordStudies.meaning: A secret plan now revealed. In Paul's usage, not something mysterious or unknowable but something previously hidden that God has now disclosed. God's secret plan to save the world through a crucified Messiah was hidden for ages but is now revealed through the Spirit.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Pertaining to the Spirit (pneuma)
wordStudies.meaning: Pertaining to the Spirit (pneuma). Paul contrasts the pneumatikos (spiritual person, animated by God's Spirit) with the psychikos (natural/soulish person, operating only on human faculties). The difference is not intelligence but the presence or absence of the Spirit's illumination.

verses.text: When I came to you, brothers, I didn’t come with excellence of speech or of wisdom, proclaiming to you the testimony of God.
verses.explainer: Paul reminds them that he came to Corinth without 'excellence of speech or of wisdom.' In a city that prized rhetorical brilliance, Paul deliberately presented himself as unimpressive. This was a strategic choice, not a personal deficiency.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Rhetorical culture in Corinth', 'explanation': "Corinth was saturated with professional orators (sophists) who competed for audiences and charged fees for their eloquence. Public speaking was entertainment, education, and social currency. Paul's refusal to compete on these terms was a deliberate rejection of this entire system."}

verses.text: For I determined not to know anything among you, except Jesus Christ, and him crucified.
verses.explainer: Paul resolved to know nothing among them except Jesus Christ and him crucified. He narrowed his message to its most offensive, least marketable core. No philosophical flourishes, no crowd-pleasing rhetoric -- just the raw scandal of a crucified savior.

verses.text: I was with you in weakness, in fear, and in much trembling.
verses.explainer: Paul was with them 'in weakness, fear, and much trembling.' This is a startling admission. The great apostle was scared. His vulnerability makes the effectiveness of his preaching all the more clearly God's doing, not his own.

thenVsNow.then: Greco-Roman culture valued bold, confident self-presentation. Admitting weakness or fear would have been social suicide for a public figure. Paul's honesty was countercultural and risked losing his audience's respect.
thenVsNow.now: Leaders who admit vulnerability are sometimes seen as more authentic today, but the pressure to project confidence and competence remains strong. Paul's example challenges the 'strong leader' archetype.

verses.text: My speech and my preaching were not in persuasive words of human wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power,
verses.explainer: Paul's speech and preaching were not with 'persuasive words of wisdom' but with 'demonstration of the Spirit and of power.' The proof of his message was not its eloquence but its effect -- lives transformed, demons expelled, communities formed.

verses.text: that your faith wouldn’t stand in the wisdom of men, but in the power of God.
verses.explainer: The purpose: so that their faith would rest not on human wisdom but on God's power. If Paul had won them over with brilliant rhetoric, their faith would depend on his speaking ability. By preaching plainly, he ensured their faith was grounded in something more durable.

verses.text: We speak wisdom, however, among those who are full grown; yet a wisdom not of this world, nor of the rulers of this world, who are coming to nothing.
verses.explainer: Paul pivots: he does speak wisdom, but among the mature -- not the wisdom of this age or its rulers, who are passing away. There is a deeper wisdom available, but it is not what the Corinthians have been chasing.

verses.text: But we speak God’s wisdom in a mystery, the wisdom that has been hidden, which God foreordained before the worlds for our glory,
verses.explainer: God's wisdom is a mystery -- a secret plan hidden for ages but now revealed. This mystery is not esoteric knowledge for an elite few but God's plan to save the world through the cross, now made available to all who have the Spirit.

verses.text: which none of the rulers of this world has known. For had they known it, they wouldn’t have crucified the Lord of glory.
verses.explainer: None of the rulers of this age understood God's wisdom. If they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory. The crucifixion, which appeared to be the rulers' greatest victory, was actually their greatest blunder -- they destroyed themselves by destroying the one who came to save.

verses.text:
But as it is written,
“Things which an eye didn’t see, and an ear didn’t hear,
which didn’t enter into the heart of man,
these God has prepared for those who love him.”
verses.explainer: Paul loosely quotes Isaiah 64:4: no eye has seen, no ear has heard, no mind has conceived what God has prepared for those who love him. The things God has planned exceed the capacity of human senses and imagination.

verses.text: But to us, God revealed them through the Spirit. For the Spirit searches all things, yes, the deep things of God.
verses.explainer: But God has revealed these things to us through the Spirit. What human faculties cannot reach, the Spirit delivers directly. The Spirit is the bridge between God's hidden wisdom and human understanding.

thenVsNow.then: In the Greco-Roman world, access to hidden knowledge (gnosis) was highly prized. Mystery religions promised secret revelations to initiates. Paul co-opts this language but democratizes it -- the Spirit reveals God's wisdom to all believers, not just an elite.
thenVsNow.now: The idea that spiritual understanding requires something beyond raw intellect -- that the Spirit must illuminate truth -- remains foundational to Christian epistemology. It challenges both pure rationalism and pure emotionalism.

verses.text: For who among men knows the things of a man, except the spirit of the man, which is in him? Even so, no one knows the things of God, except God’s Spirit.
verses.explainer: Paul uses an analogy: only a person's own spirit knows their inner thoughts. Similarly, only God's Spirit knows the deep things of God. You cannot access God's mind from the outside; you need the Spirit as an insider.

verses.text: But we received, not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit which is from God, that we might know the things that were freely given to us by God.
verses.explainer: We have received the Spirit who is from God, not the spirit of the world. The purpose: so that we might understand the things freely given to us by God. The Spirit is not a bonus gift for super-Christians; it is the basic means by which any believer grasps what God has done.

verses.text: Which things also we speak, not in words which man’s wisdom teaches, but which the Holy Spirit teaches, comparing spiritual things with spiritual things.
verses.explainer: Paul teaches in words taught by the Spirit, combining spiritual truths with spiritual words. This is not a claim to dictation-style inspiration but an acknowledgment that communicating God's wisdom requires God's help at every level -- content and expression.

verses.text: Now the natural man doesn’t receive the things of God’s Spirit, for they are foolishness to him, and he can’t know them, because they are spiritually discerned.
verses.explainer: The natural person (psychikos) does not accept the things of the Spirit because they are spiritually discerned. This is not an insult to intelligence but a statement about categories: spiritual realities require spiritual faculties, just as music requires ears and light requires eyes.

verses.text: But he who is spiritual discerns all things, and he himself is judged by no one.
verses.explainer: The spiritual person (pneumatikos) discerns all things but is not subject to anyone else's judgment. This is not a claim to infallibility but a statement that the Spirit-filled person has access to a dimension of reality that the natural person cannot evaluate.

verses.text:
“For who has known the mind of the Lord, that he should instruct him?”
 But we have Christ’s mind.
verses.explainer: Paul concludes with a question from Isaiah 40:13 -- 'Who has known the mind of the Lord?' -- and then makes an astonishing claim: 'We have the mind of Christ.' Through the Spirit, believers have access to Christ's own perspective on reality.

explainer.summary: Paul confronts the Corinthians directly: despite their spiritual gifts, they are still behaving like infants. Their jealousy and quarreling prove they are worldly, not spiritual. He redefines the role of leaders like himself and Apollos -- they are servants, not celebrities. Using two metaphors (a field and a building), Paul shows that all Christian workers contribute to one project, and the quality of their work will be tested by fire. He concludes with a solemn warning: the church is God's temple, and anyone who destroys it faces destruction.
themes: Christian leaders as servants, not celebrities
explainer.contextInBook: Paul moves from the theological argument (chs. 1-2) to direct application. The Corinthians cannot claim to have the Spirit while behaving like pagans. Their factionalism proves their immaturity.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The inner sanctuary where God dwells, not the enti
wordStudies.meaning: The inner sanctuary where God dwells, not the entire temple complex (hieron). Paul uses naos to say the church community -- not the building but the people together -- is the holy of holies where God's Spirit resides. Destroying the community is desecrating God's sanctuary.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The base upon which a structure is built
wordStudies.meaning: The base upon which a structure is built. Paul insists there is only one foundation: Jesus Christ. Any ministry or teaching must be built on him, or it will not survive the fire of judgment.

verses.text: Brothers, I couldn’t speak to you as to spiritual, but as to fleshly, as to babies in Christ.
verses.explainer: Paul could not address them as 'spiritual' but as 'fleshly' -- mere infants in Christ. Despite their impressive gifts, their behavior reveals spiritual immaturity. Gifts and maturity are not the same thing.

verses.text: I fed you with milk, not with meat; for you weren’t yet ready. Indeed, not even now are you ready,
verses.explainer: Paul fed them milk, not solid food, because they were not ready. And they are still not ready. This is not a compliment about their early growth but a rebuke about their continued stunting.

verses.text: for you are still fleshly. For insofar as there is jealousy, strife, and factions among you, aren’t you fleshly, and don’t you walk in the ways of men?
verses.explainer: The proof of their immaturity: jealousy and quarreling. These are 'fleshly' behaviors. If they were truly spiritual, these rivalries would be unthinkable. Spiritual maturity is measured by relational health, not by theological knowledge or charismatic gifts.

thenVsNow.then: Greco-Roman culture was intensely competitive. Social advancement depended on outdoing rivals. The Corinthians brought this competitive mentality into the church.
thenVsNow.now: Church splits, denominational rivalries, and online theological arguments often display exactly the same pattern Paul diagnoses: using spiritual language to pursue worldly status.

verses.text: For when one says, “I follow Paul,” and another, “I follow Apollos,” aren’t you fleshly?
verses.explainer: When one says 'I follow Paul' and another 'I follow Apollos,' they are merely human. The factions reduce spiritual reality to human tribalism.

verses.text: Who then is Apollos, and who is Paul, but servants through whom you believed; and each as the Lord gave to him?
verses.explainer: What is Apollos? What is Paul? Servants through whom you believed. Paul deflates the celebrity pastor model. He and Apollos are not rival brands; they are waiters serving the same meal.

verses.text: I planted. Apollos watered. But God gave the increase.
verses.explainer: Paul planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth. The agricultural metaphor makes the point: the workers are important but interchangeable. The growth comes from God, not from the gardener's personal brand.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient patron-client system, you owed loyalty to whoever had done you a favor. Paul rejects this model: you owe loyalty to God, not to the human minister who happened to plant or water.
thenVsNow.now: Churches that thrive long-term tend to be those where the community's identity is rooted in Christ rather than in a founding pastor. When a charismatic leader leaves, personality-centered churches often collapse.

verses.text: So then neither he who plants is anything, nor he who waters, but God who gives the increase.
verses.explainer: Neither the one who plants nor the one who waters is anything -- God who gives the growth is everything. Paul says neither he nor Apollos matters compared to God. This is radical humility from someone who could claim significant authority.

verses.text: Now he who plants and he who waters are the same, but each will receive his own reward according to his own labor.
verses.explainer: The one who plants and the one who waters have one purpose, and each will receive their own reward according to their own labor. Workers are valued but unified. There is no hierarchy among servants; only the quality of their work matters.

verses.text: For we are God’s fellow workers. You are God’s farming, God’s building.
verses.explainer: We are God's fellow workers; you are God's field, God's building. Paul shifts to a construction metaphor. The church is not Paul's project or Apollos' project but God's. All human workers are collaborators on God's building site.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Construction in Corinth', 'explanation': "Corinth had been extensively rebuilt after its destruction by Rome in 146 BC and its refounding as a colony in 44 BC. The city was full of impressive new construction. Paul's building metaphor would have resonated with a city whose identity was tied to being built and rebuilt."}

verses.text: According to the grace of God which was given to me, as a wise master builder I laid a foundation, and another builds on it. But let each man be careful how he builds on it.
verses.explainer: Paul laid the foundation like a skilled master builder (architekton), and someone else builds on it. But each must be careful how they build. The foundation is fixed; the superstructure requires discernment.

verses.text: For no one can lay any other foundation than that which has been laid, which is Jesus Christ.
verses.explainer: No one can lay any foundation other than Jesus Christ. This is non-negotiable. Whatever ministry is built in Corinth must be built on Christ, not on a personality, a philosophy, or a cultural program.

verses.text: But if anyone builds on the foundation with gold, silver, costly stones, wood, hay, or stubble;
verses.explainer: People build on the foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, or straw. The materials represent the quality of ministry -- some will survive testing, others will not. The point is not the size of what you build but what it is made of.

verses.text: each man’s work will be revealed. For the Day will declare it, because it is revealed in fire; and the fire itself will test what sort of work each man’s work is.
verses.explainer: Each person's work will be revealed by fire on 'the Day.' The fire tests what kind of work it is. Paul envisions a future judgment where the quality -- not the quantity or popularity -- of Christian ministry is evaluated.

verses.text: If any man’s work remains which he built on it, he will receive a reward.
verses.explainer: If what someone built survives the fire, they receive a reward. Durable ministry -- built with integrity, truth, and love -- endures God's evaluation.

verses.text: If any man’s work is burned, he will suffer loss, but he himself will be saved, but as through fire.
verses.explainer: If someone's work is burned up, they suffer loss but are themselves saved -- 'yet so as through fire.' The person is saved but their life's work is destroyed. This is salvation by the skin of your teeth, rescued from the wreckage of wasted effort.

thenVsNow.then: The image of being saved 'through fire' would have evoked the experience of escaping a burning building -- alive but with nothing to show for your life's work.
thenVsNow.now: This passage is sometimes used in Catholic theology as support for the doctrine of purgatory. Protestant interpreters generally see it as a metaphor for judgment of works, not a literal purifying fire after death.

verses.text: Don’t you know that you are a temple of God, and that God’s Spirit lives in you?
verses.explainer: Do you not know that you are God's temple and that the Spirit of God lives in you? The 'you' here is plural -- the community together is the temple, not each individual. The church collectively is the dwelling place of God's Spirit.

verses.text: If anyone destroys God’s temple, God will destroy him; for God’s temple is holy, which you are.
verses.explainer: If anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy that person. This is one of Paul's harshest warnings in the entire letter. Those who tear apart the church community through factionalism are desecrating the place where God lives.

verses.text: Let no one deceive himself. If anyone thinks that he is wise among you in this world, let him become a fool, that he may become wise.
verses.explainer: Let no one deceive themselves. If anyone thinks they are wise by worldly standards, they should become a 'fool' so they can become truly wise. Paul circles back to the wisdom argument: worldly wisdom is a dead end.

verses.text: For the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God. For it is written, “He has taken the wise in their craftiness.”
verses.explainer: The wisdom of this world is foolishness with God. Paul quotes Job 5:13: God catches the wise in their craftiness. Human cleverness is no match for divine purposes.

verses.text: And again, “The Lord knows the reasoning of the wise, that it is worthless.”
verses.explainer: Paul quotes Psalm 94:11: the Lord knows the reasoning of the wise, that it is futile. Two Old Testament witnesses confirm the same point: worldly wisdom leads nowhere.

verses.text: Therefore let no one boast in men. For all things are yours,
verses.explainer: Therefore let no one boast in human leaders. Paul returns to the presenting problem -- stop making faction heroes out of apostles.

verses.text: whether Paul, or Apollos, or Cephas, or the world, or life, or death, or things present, or things to come. All are yours,
verses.explainer: Everything belongs to you: Paul, Apollos, Cephas, the world, life, death, present, future -- all of it is yours. This is a staggering reversal. The Corinthians thought they belonged to their favorite leader; Paul says all leaders belong to them.

verses.text: and you are Christ’s, and Christ is God’s.
verses.explainer: And you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God. The hierarchy is clear: everything serves the church, the church serves Christ, and Christ serves God. There is no room for human boasting in this chain of ownership.

explainer.summary: Paul concludes the factionalism argument by describing apostles as stewards of God's mysteries who answer to God alone, not to human evaluation. He uses biting sarcasm to contrast the Corinthians' sense of spiritual superiority with the actual suffering of the apostles. The chapter ends with a tender but firm appeal: Paul is their spiritual father, and he is sending Timothy to remind them of his ways. He warns that he will come soon, and they can choose whether he comes with a rod of discipline or with love and gentleness.
themes: The suffering and humiliation of apostles
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter wraps up the first major section of the letter (chs. 1-4). Paul has demolished the Corinthians' basis for factionalism -- worldly wisdom, personality cults, and self-congratulation. Now he models the alternative: faithful, humble service that does not seek human approval.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Household managers
wordStudies.meaning: Household managers. A steward was a slave or freedperson who managed the master's property and affairs. The steward had significant authority but no ownership -- everything belonged to the master. Paul and the other apostles are stewards of God's message, not owners of it.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A theater or public exhibition
wordStudies.meaning: A theater or public exhibition. In the arena, condemned criminals were paraded before the crowd as the final act (the least honored). Paul says apostles are displayed last, like doomed gladiators, for the amusement of the universe. The word 'theater' comes directly from this Greek root.

verses.text: So let a man think of us as Christ’s servants, and stewards of God’s mysteries.
verses.explainer: People should regard apostles as servants of Christ and stewards of God's mysteries. The word 'servant' here is hyperetes -- an under-rower, the lowest rank on a galley ship. Paul places himself at the bottom of the hierarchy, not the top.

verses.text: Here, moreover, it is required of stewards, that they be found faithful.
verses.explainer: The primary requirement of stewards is faithfulness, not success, popularity, or brilliance. A steward answers to the master, not to the other servants or the public.

verses.text: But with me it is a very small thing that I should be judged by you, or by man’s judgment. Yes, I don’t judge my own self.
verses.explainer: Paul says it is a 'very small thing' to be judged by the Corinthians or by any human court. He does not even judge himself. Human evaluation -- including self-evaluation -- is unreliable.

thenVsNow.then: In the Greco-Roman world, public reputation was everything. A person's honor rating determined their social standing, business opportunities, and political influence. Paul's dismissal of human judgment was radical.
thenVsNow.now: In an age of online reviews, social media metrics, and performance evaluations, the idea that human judgment is ultimately irrelevant remains deeply countercultural.

verses.text: For I know nothing against myself. Yet I am not justified by this, but he who judges me is the Lord.
verses.explainer: Paul knows of nothing against himself, but that does not make him innocent. The one who judges him is the Lord. Even a clear conscience is not definitive -- only God's assessment matters.

verses.text: Therefore judge nothing before the time, until the Lord comes, who will both bring to light the hidden things of darkness, and reveal the counsels of the hearts. Then each man will get his praise from God.
verses.explainer: Do not judge before the time -- wait until the Lord comes. He will bring to light hidden things and reveal the motives of hearts. Then each person will receive their praise from God. Final evaluation belongs to God alone, and it considers motives, not just outcomes.

verses.text: Now these things, brothers, I have in a figure transferred to myself and Apollos for your sakes, that in us you might learn not to think beyond the things which are written, that none of you be puffed up against one another.
verses.explainer: Paul says he has applied all this to himself and Apollos 'for your benefit.' The entire argument about servants and stewards was designed to deflate the personality cults. Learn from their example not to go beyond what is written or to be puffed up in favor of one against another.

verses.text: For who makes you different? And what do you have that you didn’t receive? But if you did receive it, why do you boast as if you had not received it?
verses.explainer: Three devastating questions: Who makes you different from anyone else? What do you have that you did not receive? And if you received it, why do you boast as if you did not? Everything the Corinthians have is a gift. Gifts cannot be the basis for boasting.

thenVsNow.then: Corinth was a meritocratic Roman colony where self-made men were common. Social climbers who had earned their status were proud of it. Paul says even spiritual status is a gift, not an achievement.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between meritocracy and grace remains central to Christian ethics. If everything good in my life is ultimately a gift, the entire framework of earned superiority collapses.

verses.text: You are already filled. You have already become rich. You have come to reign without us. Yes, and I wish that you did reign, that we also might reign with you.
verses.explainer: Now the sarcasm begins: 'Already you have all you want! Already you have become rich! You have begun to reign -- and that without us!' Paul mockingly pretends to congratulate them on having achieved spiritual perfection without any further apostolic help.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Corinthian self-satisfaction', 'explanation': "Some Corinthians apparently believed they had already entered a fully realized spiritual kingdom. They considered themselves spiritually complete, already reigning with Christ. Paul's sarcasm targets this 'over-realized eschatology' -- acting as if the final victory has already fully arrived."}

verses.text: For, I think that God has displayed us, the apostles, last of all, like men sentenced to death. For we are made a spectacle to the world, both to angels and men.
verses.explainer: Paul contrasts their self-satisfied 'reigning' with the apostles' actual experience: God has displayed them last, like condemned prisoners in the arena, a spectacle to the whole universe -- angels and humans alike.

verses.text: We are fools for Christ’s sake, but you are wise in Christ. We are weak, but you are strong. You have honor, but we have dishonor.
verses.explainer: 'We are fools for Christ, but you are wise in Christ! We are weak, but you are strong! You are honored, we are dishonored!' The sarcasm is thick. The Corinthians enjoy comfort and prestige while the apostles who brought them the gospel suffer humiliation.

verses.text: Even to this present hour we hunger, thirst, are naked, are beaten, and have no certain dwelling place.
verses.explainer: To this present hour, the apostles are hungry, thirsty, poorly dressed, beaten, and homeless. This is not abstract theology but Paul's actual lived experience. Apostleship means suffering, not status.

thenVsNow.then: The ancient world associated divine favor with prosperity and comfort. A messenger from God should look successful. Paul's suffering was evidence, to some, that he was not a legitimate apostle.
thenVsNow.now: The prosperity gospel -- the idea that God rewards faithful believers with health and wealth -- faces the same challenge from Paul's example. The greatest apostle was often homeless and hungry.

verses.text: We toil, working with our own hands. When people curse us, we bless. Being persecuted, we endure.
verses.explainer: Paul works with his own hands -- manual labor, which was despised by the educated class. When reviled, he blesses. When persecuted, he endures. He embodies the upside-down values of the cross.

verses.text: Being defamed, we entreat. We are made as the filth of the world, the dirt wiped off by all, even until now.
verses.explainer: Apostles are treated as the refuse of the world, the scum of all things. The Greek word peripsema (scum) referred to what was scraped off the bottom of a pot or to human scapegoats sacrificed to appease the gods.

verses.text: I don’t write these things to shame you, but to admonish you as my beloved children.
verses.explainer: Paul's tone shifts abruptly from sarcasm to tenderness: 'I do not write these things to shame you but to admonish you as my beloved children.' The rebuke was not meant to wound but to awaken.

verses.text: For though you have ten thousand tutors in Christ, you don’t have many fathers. For in Christ Jesus, I became your father through the Good News.
verses.explainer: Even if they have ten thousand instructors in Christ, they do not have many fathers. Paul became their father through the gospel -- he is the one who brought them to faith. This gives him a unique authority that no subsequent teacher can claim.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Paidagogoi versus fathers', 'explanation': "A paidagogos was a slave who supervised a child's behavior and walked them to school. They were common but not particularly esteemed. Paul contrasts the many paidagogoi the Corinthians might acquire with his unique fatherly role -- other teachers guide, but he gave them spiritual life."}

verses.text: I beg you therefore, be imitators of me.
verses.explainer: Therefore, Paul urges them: imitate me. This is not arrogance but the confidence of a parent telling their children to follow his example. The apostle's life is the sermon.

verses.text: Because of this I have sent Timothy to you, who is my beloved and faithful child in the Lord, who will remind you of my ways which are in Christ, even as I teach everywhere in every assembly.
verses.explainer: Paul is sending Timothy, his beloved and faithful child in the Lord, to remind them of Paul's ways in Christ. Timothy will model and teach the same pattern of humble, cruciform living that Paul practices everywhere.

verses.text: Now some are puffed up, as though I were not coming to you.
verses.explainer: Some Corinthians have become arrogant, assuming Paul would not return. They are taking advantage of his absence to consolidate their own power and influence.

verses.text: But I will come to you shortly, if the Lord is willing. And I will know, not the word of those who are puffed up, but the power.
verses.explainer: But Paul will come soon, if the Lord wills. And when he does, he will test not the 'talk' of the arrogant but their 'power.' Rhetoric is easy; genuine spiritual power is what matters.

verses.text: For God’s Kingdom is not in word, but in power.
verses.explainer: The kingdom of God does not consist in talk but in power. This is a direct hit at the Corinthian obsession with eloquent speech. Words are cheap; transformed lives are the real evidence of the kingdom.

verses.text: What do you want? Shall I come to you with a rod, or in love and a spirit of gentleness?
verses.explainer: Paul offers a choice: 'Shall I come to you with a rod, or with love in a spirit of gentleness?' The decision is theirs. If they repent and get their house in order, he comes as a loving father. If they persist in arrogance, he comes with discipline.

thenVsNow.then: A Roman paterfamilias had absolute authority over his household, including the right to discipline. Paul invokes this cultural framework while tempering it with the option of gentleness.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between love and accountability in Christian leadership remains unresolved in many churches. Paul models a combination that modern leaders often struggle to balance: genuine affection paired with willingness to confront.

explainer.summary: Paul addresses a shocking case: a man in the church is sleeping with his father's wife (his stepmother), and the congregation is proud of their tolerance rather than grieved. Paul orders them to expel the man -- not out of cruelty but to shock him into repentance and to protect the community from moral corruption. He uses the metaphor of Passover leaven: a little yeast spreads through the whole batch of dough. The church must clean house.
themes: The danger of tolerance that becomes complicity
explainer.contextInBook: Paul shifts from the factionalism problem (chs. 1-4) to the moral crisis. The Corinthians' distorted understanding of freedom and wisdom has led them to tolerate behavior that even pagans find repulsive.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Yeast or sourdough starter
wordStudies.meaning: Yeast or sourdough starter. In Jewish tradition, leaven symbolized corruption and moral contamination. Before Passover, every Jewish household was required to remove all leaven. Paul uses this to argue that tolerating sin in the church is like leaving leaven in the dough -- it will spread.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A dramatic phrase for excommunication -- putting s
wordStudies.meaning: A dramatic phrase for excommunication -- putting someone outside the church community and into the realm of Satan (the world). The purpose was remedial: physical suffering in the world was meant to drive the person back to repentance.

verses.text: It is actually reported that there is sexual immorality among you, and such sexual immorality as is not even named among the Gentiles, that one has his father’s wife.
verses.explainer: Sexual immorality is reported among them -- and of a kind that does not occur even among pagans: a man has his father's wife. Both Roman law and Jewish law prohibited this relationship. The Corinthians have not just matched pagan morality; they have fallen below it.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Father's wife", 'explanation': "This almost certainly means the man's stepmother, not his biological mother. Both Jewish law (Leviticus 18:8) and Roman law (the Lex Julia de adulteriis) prohibited sexual relations with a stepmother. This was not a gray area in any culture of the time."}

verses.text: You are puffed up, and didn’t rather mourn, that he who had done this deed might be removed from among you.
verses.explainer: Instead of mourning, the Corinthians are 'puffed up.' They may have been proud of their supposed spiritual maturity and freedom -- so advanced that they could tolerate anything. Paul sees this not as maturity but as moral blindness.

thenVsNow.then: Some Corinthians may have interpreted Paul's teaching about freedom in Christ to mean that moral rules no longer applied. If the body does not matter (a Greco-Roman philosophical idea), then what you do with it is irrelevant.
thenVsNow.now: The tension between grace and moral standards remains live in every generation. Churches that emphasize acceptance can drift into tolerating harmful behavior; churches that emphasize holiness can drift into legalism. Paul insists on both grace and accountability.

verses.text: For I most certainly, as being absent in body but present in spirit, have already, as though I were present, judged him who has done this thing.
verses.explainer: Paul has already judged this case, even though he is physically absent. His apostolic authority operates across distance. The verdict is in before the trial.

verses.text: In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, you being gathered together, and my spirit, with the power of our Lord Jesus Christ,
verses.explainer: When the church assembles in the name of the Lord Jesus, with Paul's spirit present and the power of the Lord Jesus, they are to act. The discipline is a communal act of the gathered church, not a private decision.

verses.text: are to deliver such a one to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord Jesus.
verses.explainer: They are to 'hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved on the day of the Lord.' Excommunication puts the person outside the protective boundary of the church community. The goal is ultimately redemptive -- suffering that leads to repentance.

verses.text: Your boasting is not good. Don’t you know that a little yeast leavens the whole lump?
verses.explainer: Their boasting is not good. A little leaven leavens the whole lump. Tolerating this sin will not contain it; it will spread through the community like yeast through dough.

verses.text: Purge out the old yeast, that you may be a new lump, even as you are unleavened. For indeed Christ, our Passover, has been sacrificed in our place.
verses.explainer: Clean out the old leaven so you may be a new lump, as you really are unleavened. Paul draws on the Passover tradition: before the feast, every trace of leaven must be removed from the house. The church must do the same with moral corruption.

verses.text: Therefore let us keep the feast, not with old yeast, neither with the yeast of malice and wickedness, but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.
verses.explainer: Let us celebrate the festival -- not with old leaven of malice and evil but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth. The Christian life is a perpetual Passover: a community perpetually cleansing itself of corruption.

verses.text: I wrote to you in my letter to have no company with sexual sinners;
verses.explainer: Paul refers to a previous letter -- now lost -- in which he told them not to associate with sexually immoral people. This means 1 Corinthians is not actually the first letter Paul wrote to Corinth; there was an earlier one that has not survived.

verses.text: yet not at all meaning with the sexual sinners of this world, or with the covetous and extortionists, or with idolaters; for then you would have to leave the world.
verses.explainer: Paul clarifies: he did not mean avoid all immoral people in the wider world -- that would require leaving the planet. Christians must live in the world and interact with non-believers of every kind.

verses.text: But as it is, I wrote to you not to associate with anyone who is called a brother who is a sexual sinner, or covetous, or an idolater, or a slanderer, or a drunkard, or an extortionist. Don’t even eat with such a person.
verses.explainer: But if someone who claims to be a brother or sister is sexually immoral, greedy, an idolater, a slanderer, a drunkard, or a swindler -- do not even eat with them. The standard is higher for insiders than outsiders. The church community holds its members accountable.

thenVsNow.then: Refusing to eat with someone was the most severe social sanction available. In a culture where meals were the primary expression of community, exclusion from the table meant exclusion from the group.
thenVsNow.now: Churches struggle with how to practice accountability without becoming judgmental. Paul's principle -- higher standards for insiders, grace for outsiders -- is often reversed in practice: churches judge outsiders while tolerating anything among members.

verses.text: For what do I have to do with also judging those who are outside? Don’t you judge those who are within?
verses.explainer: Paul draws a sharp boundary: 'What business is it of mine to judge outsiders? Are you not to judge those inside?' The church's moral jurisdiction extends only to its own members.

verses.text: But those who are outside, God judges. “Put away the wicked man from among yourselves.”
verses.explainer: God judges those outside. 'Expel the wicked person from among you.' Paul quotes Deuteronomy's refrain for purging evil from Israel and applies it to the church community.

explainer.summary: Paul addresses two more problems. First, Corinthian believers are suing each other in pagan courts -- airing the church's dirty laundry before unbelieving judges. Paul is incredulous: people who will judge angels cannot settle petty disputes among themselves? Second, Paul tackles sexual immorality more broadly, demolishing the Corinthian slogan 'Everything is permissible for me.' He argues that the body matters eternally -- it is a temple of the Holy Spirit, bought at a price. Sexual sin is not just a rule violation; it is a desecration of God's dwelling.
themes: The body is for the Lord
The body as a temple of the Holy Spirit
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 6 addresses two more practical failures that stem from the Corinthians' distorted view of wisdom and freedom. Their lawsuits reflect worldly values (winning at all costs), and their sexual permissiveness reflects a Greek philosophical disdain for the physical body.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Here Paul shifts the temple metaphor from the comm
wordStudies.meaning: Here Paul shifts the temple metaphor from the community (3:16) to the individual body. Each believer's body is a naos -- the inner sanctuary where the Holy Spirit dwells. This gives the physical body a sacred status that Greek philosophy generally denied.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A marketplace term for purchasing something
wordStudies.meaning: A marketplace term for purchasing something. The imagery may come from the slave market or the temple practice of sacred manumission, where a slave was 'purchased' by a god and thereby freed from their human owner. Believers were purchased by Christ's death and now belong to God.

verses.text: Dare any of you, having a matter against his neighbor, go to law before the unrighteous, and not before the saints?
verses.explainer: Paul is astonished that believers dare to take disputes before 'the unrighteous' -- pagan judges -- instead of before the saints. The Roman court system was notoriously corrupt and favored the wealthy. Christian disputes should be resolved internally.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Roman courts', 'explanation': 'Roman legal proceedings were public spectacles where social status heavily influenced outcomes. Wealthy patrons could buy favorable verdicts. For Christians to parade their conflicts before such a system brought shame on the gospel and disadvantaged poorer church members.'}

verses.text: Don’t you know that the saints will judge the world? And if the world is judged by you, are you unworthy to judge the smallest matters?
verses.explainer: Do you not know that the saints will judge the world? If the world is subject to your judgment, are you not competent to judge trivial matters? Paul appeals to eschatology: believers have a future role that dwarfs any present legal dispute.

verses.text: Don’t you know that we will judge angels? How much more, things that pertain to this life?
verses.explainer: Do you not know that we will judge angels? How much more the affairs of this life? If believers will adjudicate cosmic matters, surely they can handle property disputes.

verses.text: If then, you have to judge things pertaining to this life, do you set them to judge who are of no account in the assembly?
verses.explainer: If you have disputes about such matters, do you appoint as judges those who have no standing in the church? The pagan courts have no business adjudicating the internal affairs of God's community.

verses.text: I say this to move you to shame. Isn’t there even one wise man among you who would be able to decide between his brothers?
verses.explainer: Paul says this 'to shame' them. Is there not one wise person among them who can arbitrate? The Corinthians pride themselves on wisdom (chs. 1-4), yet cannot produce a single person wise enough to settle a dispute.

verses.text: But brother goes to law with brother, and that before unbelievers!
verses.explainer: Instead, brother goes to law against brother -- and before unbelievers. The spectacle of Christians suing each other in public courts damages the church's witness.

verses.text: Therefore it is already altogether a defect in you, that you have lawsuits one with another. Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?
verses.explainer: The very fact that you have lawsuits among you means you have already been defeated. Paul makes a radical claim: it is better to be wronged and cheated than to take a fellow believer to court.

thenVsNow.then: Roman society prized asserting one's legal rights. Failing to sue when wronged was seen as weakness. Paul's call to accept injustice was deeply countercultural.
thenVsNow.now: The question of when Christians should and should not use the legal system remains debated. Paul's principle -- preferring to absorb loss rather than damage community -- challenges the instinct to fight for every right.

verses.text: No, but you yourselves do wrong, and defraud, and that against your brothers.
verses.explainer: Instead, you yourselves cheat and do wrong -- and you do this to your brothers and sisters. The plaintiffs are not innocent victims; they are perpetrators who then compound their wrong by going to pagan courts.

verses.text: Or don’t you know that the unrighteous will not inherit God’s Kingdom? Don’t be deceived. Neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor male prostitutes, nor homosexuals,
verses.explainer: Do you not know that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived. Paul provides a vice list: the sexually immoral, idolaters, adulterers, passive and active homosexual partners, thieves, the greedy, drunkards, slanderers, swindlers. None of these will inherit the kingdom.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Vice lists', 'explanation': "Vice lists were a common literary form in both Jewish and Greco-Roman moral teaching. Paul's list combines sexual sins, economic sins, and social sins. The list is representative, not exhaustive -- it covers the major categories of behavior incompatible with God's kingdom."}

verses.text: nor thieves, nor covetous, nor drunkards, nor slanderers, nor extortionists, will inherit God’s Kingdom.
verses.explainer: The list continues: thieves, the greedy, drunkards, slanderers, swindlers. Paul expands beyond sexual sin to include economic exploitation and social harm. The kingdom excludes all forms of unrepentant wrongdoing, not just sexual ones.

verses.text: Such were some of you, but you were washed. But you were sanctified. But you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus, and in the Spirit of our God.
verses.explainer: And that is what some of you were. But you were washed, sanctified, and justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of God. This is one of the most powerful sentences in Paul's letters. Past tense: 'you were.' Present reality: you have been transformed. The gospel changed them; they must not go back.

thenVsNow.then: Many Corinthian believers came from the very backgrounds Paul lists. The early church was not a gathering of respectable people but a community of transformed sinners. This made the church radically inclusive while demanding radical change.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to debates about Christianity and moral transformation. It asserts both that people can change and that genuine faith produces actual change -- not perfection, but a real departure from the old pattern.

verses.text: “All things are lawful for me,” but not all things are expedient. “All things are lawful for me,” but I will not be brought under the power of anything.
verses.explainer: Paul quotes a Corinthian slogan back to them: 'All things are lawful for me.' He agrees -- but adds two qualifications: not everything is beneficial, and he will not be mastered by anything. Freedom is real, but it has limits: usefulness and self-control.

verses.text: “Foods for the belly, and the belly for foods,” but God will bring to nothing both it and them. But the body is not for sexual immorality, but for the Lord; and the Lord for the body.
verses.explainer: 'Food for the stomach and the stomach for food, and God will destroy them both.' Another Corinthian slogan, arguing that physical activities (eating, sex) are morally neutral because the body is temporary. Paul partially agrees about food but completely rejects the analogy to sex: the body is for the Lord, and the Lord for the body.

verses.text: Now God raised up the Lord, and will also raise us up by his power.
verses.explainer: God raised the Lord and will also raise us by his power. The body is not disposable -- God will resurrect it. This means what you do with your body matters eternally.

verses.text: Don’t you know that your bodies are members of Christ? Shall I then take the members of Christ, and make them members of a prostitute? May it never be!
verses.explainer: Your bodies are members of Christ. Shall I take the members of Christ and unite them with a prostitute? Never! Sexual union involves the whole person, not just the body. Joining Christ's body to a prostitute's body drags Christ into the transaction.

verses.text: Or don’t you know that he who is joined to a prostitute is one body? For, “The two”, he says, “will become one flesh.”
verses.explainer: The one who joins himself to a prostitute becomes one body with her. Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 -- 'the two shall become one flesh.' Sexual union creates a real, not merely symbolic, bond between two people.

verses.text: But he who is joined to the Lord is one spirit.
verses.explainer: But the one who is joined to the Lord is one spirit with him. The spiritual union with Christ is even deeper than sexual union. These two bonds cannot coexist with a prostitute.

verses.text: Flee sexual immorality! “Every sin that a man does is outside the body,” but he who commits sexual immorality sins against his own body.
verses.explainer: Flee sexual immorality. Every other sin a person commits is outside the body, but the sexually immoral person sins against their own body. Paul's argument: sexual sin is unique because it involves the body at the deepest level of personal identity.

verses.text: Or don’t you know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit who is in you, whom you have from God? You are not your own,
verses.explainer: Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit, who is in you, whom you have from God? You are not your own. This is one of the most quoted verses in the letter. The individual body, not just the church community, is where God dwells.

thenVsNow.then: Greek philosophy generally viewed the body as a prison for the soul -- something to be escaped, not honored. Paul radically revalues the physical body: it is not a prison but a temple.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is invoked in discussions about health, substance abuse, sexual ethics, and body image. The core principle -- your body has sacred significance because God inhabits it -- challenges both hedonism and self-hatred.

verses.text: for you were bought with a price. Therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit, which are God’s.
verses.explainer: You were bought with a price. Therefore glorify God in your body. The purchase metaphor evokes the slave market or sacred manumission. Believers have been bought out of slavery to sin and now belong to God. The body is the primary venue for honoring God -- not an obstacle to spirituality but the instrument of it.

explainer.summary: Paul responds to questions the Corinthians sent him in a letter ('Now concerning the things about which you wrote'). The major topic is marriage and singleness. Some Corinthians apparently wanted to ban sex entirely, even within marriage; others wanted to divorce unbelieving spouses. Paul navigates a middle path: marriage is good, singleness is good, and the current situation (the 'present distress') makes each person's calling more urgent. His overarching principle is 'remain as you are' -- do not make dramatic life changes just because you became a Christian.
themes: The present distress and eschatological urgency
explainer.contextInBook: This is the first of several sections where Paul directly answers questions from the Corinthians' letter. The phrase 'now concerning' (peri de) marks each new topic: marriage (ch. 7), food offered to idols (ch. 8), spiritual gifts (ch. 12), the collection (ch. 16).

wordStudies.translatedAs: A grace-gift from God
wordStudies.meaning: A grace-gift from God. Paul applies this word to both marriage and singleness -- each is a divine gift suited to different people. Neither is morally superior; both are ways of serving God faithfully.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The state or situation in which God called you
wordStudies.meaning: The state or situation in which God called you. Paul's radical idea: your social circumstances at the time of conversion are not obstacles to spiritual life. God meets you where you are -- married, single, slave, free, circumcised, uncircumcised.

verses.text: Now concerning the things about which you wrote to me: it is good for a man not to touch a woman.
verses.explainer: Paul addresses their letter: 'It is good for a man not to touch a woman.' This is likely a Corinthian slogan that Paul is quoting, not his own view. Some Corinthians had become so 'spiritual' that they wanted to eliminate sex entirely, even within marriage.

verses.text: But, because of sexual immoralities, let each man have his own wife, and let each woman have her own husband.
verses.explainer: But because of sexual immorality, let each man have his own wife and each woman her own husband. Paul's pragmatism: in a sexually charged city like Corinth, marriage provides a legitimate outlet. This is not the highest view of marriage (Paul offers a higher one in Ephesians 5), but it is the practical one for this context.

verses.text: Let the husband give his wife the affection owed her, and likewise also the wife her husband.
verses.explainer: Let the husband give to his wife her conjugal rights, and likewise the wife to her husband. The mutuality here is remarkable for the ancient world. Both spouses have equal rights and equal obligations in the sexual relationship.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Mutual conjugal rights', 'explanation': "In Roman law, a wife had very limited rights over her husband's body.
