Paul's insistence on full mutuality -- the husband owes the wife just as much as the wife owes the husband -- was radical egalitarianism in a patriarchal culture."}

verses.text: The wife doesn’t have authority over her own body, but the husband. Likewise also the husband doesn’t have authority over his own body, but the wife.
verses.explainer: The wife does not have authority over her own body but the husband does; likewise the husband does not have authority over his own body but the wife does. This mutual authority is astonishing. Neither spouse is autonomous; each belongs to the other.

thenVsNow.then: Roman marriage law gave the husband authority (manus) over the wife. Paul grants the wife equal authority over the husband's body -- a concept with no parallel in Roman or Jewish law.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is foundational to Christian sexual ethics within marriage: mutual self-giving, not unilateral demand. It challenges both patriarchal domination and individualistic autonomy.

verses.text: Don’t deprive one another, unless it is by consent for a season, that you may give yourselves to fasting and prayer, and may be together again, that Satan doesn’t tempt you because of your lack of self-control.
verses.explainer: Do not deprive each other except by mutual consent and for a limited time of prayer, then come together again so that Satan does not tempt you through lack of self-control. Temporary sexual abstinence for prayer is permitted, but only if both agree and only for a set period.

verses.text: But this I say by way of concession, not of commandment.
verses.explainer: Paul offers this as a concession, not a command. He is giving practical pastoral advice, not issuing divine legislation.

verses.text: Yet I wish that all men were like me. However each man has his own gift from God, one of this kind, and another of that kind.
verses.explainer: Paul wishes everyone were as he is -- single. But each has their own gift (charisma) from God, one of one kind and one of another. Paul elevates singleness without denigrating marriage. Both are charismata -- grace-gifts for different people.

thenVsNow.then: In Jewish culture, marriage was virtually mandatory. An unmarried adult male was considered incomplete and was failing the command to 'be fruitful and multiply.' Paul's positive valuation of singleness was countercultural in a Jewish context.
thenVsNow.now: Many churches unintentionally marginalize single adults by treating marriage as the norm and singleness as a problem to be solved. Paul's perspective offers a corrective: singleness is not a waiting room for marriage but a legitimate calling.

verses.text: But I say to the unmarried and to widows, it is good for them if they remain even as I am.
verses.explainer: To the unmarried and widows, Paul says it is good for them to remain as he is -- single.

verses.text: But if they don’t have self-control, let them marry. For it’s better to marry than to burn.
verses.explainer: But if they cannot exercise self-control, let them marry. It is better to marry than to burn with passion. Paul is practical, not idealistic. He prefers singleness but recognizes it is not everyone's gift.

verses.text: But to the married I command—not I, but the Lord—that the wife not leave her husband
verses.explainer: To the married, Paul gives a charge -- not his own but the Lord's: a wife must not separate from her husband. This refers to Jesus' teaching on divorce (Mark 10:9-12), which Paul explicitly identifies as coming from Christ, not from himself.

verses.text: (but if she departs, let her remain unmarried, or else be reconciled to her husband), and that the husband not leave his wife.
verses.explainer: But if she does separate, she must remain unmarried or be reconciled to her husband. And a husband must not divorce his wife. Paul acknowledges that separation happens but maintains that remarriage is not an option -- reconciliation is.

verses.text: But to the rest I—not the Lord—say, if any brother has an unbelieving wife, and she is content to live with him, let him not leave her.
verses.explainer: To the rest -- meaning believers married to unbelievers -- Paul speaks on his own authority, not quoting Jesus directly. He distinguishes between Jesus' teaching and his own apostolic guidance. This is a remarkable act of intellectual honesty.

verses.text: The woman who has an unbelieving husband, and he is content to live with her, let her not leave her husband.
verses.explainer: If a woman has an unbelieving husband and he consents to live with her, she should not divorce him. The believing spouse should not initiate divorce just because their partner is not a Christian.

verses.text: For the unbelieving husband is sanctified in the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified in the husband. Otherwise your children would be unclean, but now they are holy.
verses.explainer: The unbelieving husband is sanctified through the wife, and the unbelieving wife through the husband. Otherwise your children would be unclean, but as it is, they are holy. The believing spouse's faith creates a zone of sanctification around the entire household.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Sanctification of the household', 'explanation': "In the ancient world, the household was the basic social unit. Paul argues that one believer in a household brings the whole family within the sphere of God's grace -- not that unbelieving family members are automatically saved, but that they are set apart in a special relationship to the community of faith."}

verses.text: Yet if the unbeliever departs, let there be separation. The brother or the sister is not under bondage in such cases, but God has called us in peace.
verses.explainer: But if the unbeliever leaves, let them go. The brother or sister is not bound in such cases. God has called us to peace. This is the 'Pauline privilege' -- if an unbelieving spouse abandons the marriage, the believer is free.

thenVsNow.then: Divorce was common in the Roman world but stigmatized in Jewish culture. Paul navigates between these worlds: marriage should be preserved, but peace and freedom matter when an unbeliever walks away.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to pastoral discussions about divorce and remarriage. The 'Pauline privilege' is recognized in Catholic canon law and shapes Protestant pastoral practice in cases of abandonment.

verses.text: For how do you know, wife, whether you will save your husband? Or how do you know, husband, whether you will save your wife?
verses.explainer: How do you know, wife, whether you will save your husband? Or husband, whether you will save your wife? The question cuts both ways -- there is hope for the unbeliever's conversion, but no guarantee. Do not stay in a destructive situation based on uncertain hope.

verses.text: Only, as the Lord has distributed to each man, as God has called each, so let him walk. So I command in all the assemblies.
verses.explainer: Paul states his general principle: each person should live as the Lord has assigned, in the situation God called them. This is not fatalism but contentment -- do not assume conversion requires a complete overhaul of your external circumstances.

verses.text: Was anyone called having been circumcised? Let him not become uncircumcised. Has anyone been called in uncircumcision? Let him not be circumcised.
verses.explainer: Was anyone called while circumcised? He should not seek to remove the marks of circumcision. Was anyone called while uncircumcised? He should not be circumcised. Circumcision, the most visible marker of Jewish identity, is irrelevant to standing before God.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Epispasm', 'explanation': 'Some Jewish men in the Hellenistic world underwent a painful surgical procedure called epispasm to reverse circumcision, allowing them to participate in Greek athletic competitions (which were performed nude) without the social stigma of visible circumcision. Paul says not to bother.'}

verses.text: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.
verses.explainer: Circumcision is nothing and uncircumcision is nothing. Keeping the commandments of God is what matters. Paul relativizes the most divisive identity marker in the ancient Jewish world. What counts is obedience, not ethnicity.

verses.text: Let each man stay in that calling in which he was called.
verses.explainer: Each person should remain in the calling in which they were called. This is the chapter's refrain. Conversion does not require social upheaval.

verses.text: Were you called being a bondservant? Don’t let that bother you, but if you get an opportunity to become free, use it.
verses.explainer: Were you called while a slave? Do not let it trouble you. But if you can gain your freedom, do so. Paul accepts the reality of slavery without endorsing it. If freedom is available, take it -- but slavery is not a barrier to genuine Christian life.

thenVsNow.then: Slavery was pervasive in the Roman Empire -- perhaps one-third of the population of Corinth were slaves or freedpeople. Paul's advice was addressed to people who had no choice in their social status.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's failure to explicitly condemn slavery is one of the most debated aspects of his legacy. His focus on inner freedom within external bondage was pragmatic but has been criticized for being complicit with an unjust system.

verses.text: For he who was called in the Lord being a bondservant is the Lord’s free man. Likewise he who was called being free is Christ’s bondservant.
verses.explainer: The slave who is called in the Lord is the Lord's freedperson; likewise the free person who is called is Christ's slave. Paul performs a radical inversion: the slave is truly free, and the free person is truly a slave. Social status is not ultimate reality.

verses.text: You were bought with a price. Don’t become bondservants of men.
verses.explainer: You were bought with a price. Do not become slaves of human beings. The purchase metaphor returns: believers belong to Christ, and their primary allegiance is to him, not to any human master.

verses.text: Brothers, let each man, in whatever condition he was called, stay in that condition with God.
verses.explainer: Brothers and sisters, each should remain with God in the situation they were in when God called them. Paul repeats the principle a third time for emphasis.

verses.text: Now concerning virgins, I have no commandment from the Lord, but I give my judgment as one who has obtained mercy from the Lord to be trustworthy.
verses.explainer: Now concerning virgins, Paul has no command from the Lord but gives his own judgment as one who by the Lord's mercy is trustworthy. Again Paul distinguishes between Jesus' direct teaching and his own apostolic counsel.

verses.text: Therefore I think that because of the distress that is on us, that it’s good for a man to remain as he is.
verses.explainer: Because of the 'present distress,' Paul thinks it is good for a person to remain as they are. The 'present distress' may refer to a specific crisis (famine, persecution) or to the general end-times urgency Paul felt.

verses.text: Are you bound to a wife? Don’t seek to be freed. Are you free from a wife? Don’t seek a wife.
verses.explainer: Are you bound to a wife? Do not seek to be free. Are you free from a wife? Do not seek a wife. Paul advises stability in both directions.

verses.text: But if you marry, you have not sinned. If a virgin marries, she has not sinned. Yet such will have oppression in the flesh, and I want to spare you.
verses.explainer: But if you marry, you have not sinned. Marriage is not wrong; Paul just thinks it adds complications in a time of crisis.

verses.text: But I say this, brothers: the time is short, that from now on, both those who have wives may be as though they had none;
verses.explainer: The time is short. From now on, those who have wives should live as if they had none. Paul is not advocating abandonment but an inner detachment from all earthly circumstances. The end is near enough that temporary arrangements should not be treated as permanent.

thenVsNow.then: Paul expected Jesus to return soon, possibly in his lifetime. This eschatological urgency shaped his practical advice: why rearrange the furniture when the house is about to be renovated?
thenVsNow.now: Nearly two thousand years later, Jesus has not returned. This raises questions about whether Paul's advice was time-bound or permanent. Most interpreters see the principle (hold earthly things loosely) as lasting, while the specific expectation of imminent return was not fulfilled as Paul expected.

verses.text: and those who weep, as though they didn’t weep; and those who rejoice, as though they didn’t rejoice; and those who buy, as though they didn’t possess;
verses.explainer: Those who mourn as if they did not; those who rejoice as if they did not; those who buy as if they owned nothing. Paul calls for a radical inner freedom from the emotional and material grip of the present world.

verses.text: and those who use the world, as not using it to the fullest. For the mode of this world passes away.
verses.explainer: Those who use the world as if not fully engaged with it. For the present form of this world is passing away. The Greek word schema (form, outward appearance) suggests that the world's current arrangement is temporary -- a stage set that will be struck.

verses.text: But I desire to have you to be free from cares. He who is unmarried is concerned for the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord;
verses.explainer: Paul wants them free from anxieties. The unmarried person is concerned about the Lord's affairs -- how to please the Lord.

verses.text: but he who is married is concerned about the things of the world, how he may please his wife.
verses.explainer: But the married person is concerned about worldly affairs -- how to please their spouse. Paul is not demonizing marriage but acknowledging a real tension: marriage divides one's attention.

verses.text: There is also a difference between a wife and a virgin. The unmarried woman cares about the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit. But she who is married cares about the things of the world—how she may please her husband.
verses.explainer: There is a difference between a wife and a virgin. The unmarried woman is concerned about the Lord's affairs, to be holy in body and spirit. But the married woman is concerned about worldly affairs, how to please her husband.

verses.text: This I say for your own profit; not that I may ensnare you, but for that which is appropriate, and that you may attend to the Lord without distraction.
verses.explainer: Paul says this for their benefit, not to restrict them. He wants them to be devoted to the Lord without distraction. His purpose is not to create a hierarchy of holiness but to maximize focused devotion.

verses.text: But if any man thinks that he is behaving inappropriately toward his virgin, if she is past the flower of her age, and if need so requires, let him do what he desires. He doesn’t sin. Let them marry.
verses.explainer: If anyone thinks he is acting dishonorably toward his virgin, if she is past the flower of her age and it seems necessary, let him do what he wishes -- he does not sin. Let them marry. The exact situation is debated: this may concern a father deciding about his daughter's marriage, or a man and his betrothed.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Virgins and betrothal', 'explanation': "The passage is notoriously difficult. It may refer to a father's decision about his daughter's marriage (common in the ancient world), to a betrothed couple deciding whether to proceed, or to a 'spiritual marriage' where a couple lived together in celibacy. Scholars disagree."}

verses.text: But he who stands steadfast in his heart, having no urgency, but has power over his own will, and has determined in his own heart to keep his own virgin, does well.
verses.explainer: But whoever stands firm in his heart, not having necessity but having authority over his own will, and has determined in his heart to keep his virgin, does well. The one who refrains from marriage also does the right thing.

verses.text: So then both he who gives his own virgin in marriage does well, and he who doesn’t give her in marriage does better.
verses.explainer: So then, the one who marries does well, and the one who refrains from marriage does even better. Paul's preference for singleness is clear, but he refuses to mandate it.

verses.text: A wife is bound by law for as long as her husband lives; but if the husband is dead, she is free to be married to whomever she desires, only in the Lord.
verses.explainer: A wife is bound as long as her husband lives. But if her husband dies, she is free to marry anyone she wishes, only in the Lord.

verses.text: But she is happier if she stays as she is, in my judgment, and I think that I also have God’s Spirit.
verses.explainer: But in Paul's opinion, she is happier if she remains as she is. And Paul thinks he also has the Spirit of God. A final touch of dry humor: 'I think I have the Spirit of God' -- a modest understatement from the man who founded their church.

explainer.summary: Paul addresses another question from the Corinthians' letter: should Christians eat food that has been sacrificed to pagan idols? The 'knowledgeable' Corinthians argue that since idols are not real gods, the food is meaningless -- eat freely. Paul agrees in principle but introduces a crucial qualifier: knowledge puffs up, but love builds up. If your freedom causes a weaker believer to stumble, your knowledge has become a weapon. The strong must voluntarily limit their freedom out of love for the weak.
themes: Idols are nothing, but conscience matters
The strong must defer to the weak
Christian freedom voluntarily limited by love
explainer.contextInBook: This begins a three-chapter unit (8-10) on food offered to idols. Chapter 8 establishes the principle (love limits freedom), chapter 9 provides Paul's personal example, and chapter 10 adds the warning of Israel's wilderness failures.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Knowledge, especially the intellectual grasp of th
wordStudies.meaning: Knowledge, especially the intellectual grasp of theological truth. The Corinthians valued gnosis highly -- knowing that idols are not real gods. Paul agrees they are right but says knowledge without love is destructive. Raw knowledge inflates the ego; love builds the community.

wordStudies.translatedAs: An obstacle that causes someone to trip and fall
wordStudies.meaning: An obstacle that causes someone to trip and fall. Paul uses it for an action that causes a fellow believer to violate their conscience and fall into sin. Your freedom becomes a stumbling block when it leads someone else to do something they believe is wrong.

verses.text: Now concerning things sacrificed to idols: We know that we all have knowledge. Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.
verses.explainer: Now concerning food sacrificed to idols: Paul introduces the topic with the Corinthians' own claim: 'We all have knowledge.' Paul agrees -- but adds a devastating qualifier: 'Knowledge puffs up, but love builds up.' Being right is not enough if being right hurts others.

thenVsNow.then: In Corinth, meat from pagan temple sacrifices was sold in the public market (macellum) and served at banquets in temple dining rooms. Eating it was deeply woven into social and business life. Refusing could mean social isolation.
thenVsNow.now: The specific issue of idol meat is gone, but the principle -- should my freedom be limited by another person's conscience? -- applies to countless modern situations: alcohol around recovering addicts, lifestyle choices that scandalize fellow believers, cultural practices that mean different things to different people.

verses.text: But if anyone thinks that he knows anything, he doesn’t yet know as he ought to know.
verses.explainer: If anyone thinks they know something, they do not yet know as they ought to know. Knowledge that produces arrogance is actually ignorance in disguise. True knowledge includes knowing the limits of knowledge.

verses.text: But if anyone loves God, the same is known by him.
verses.explainer: But if anyone loves God, they are known by him. Paul pivots from what we know to being known by God. The relationship matters more than the information.

verses.text: Therefore concerning the eating of things sacrificed to idols, we know that no idol is anything in the world, and that there is no other God but one.
verses.explainer: Concerning eating food offered to idols: we know that an idol is nothing in the world and that there is no God but one. Paul affirms strict monotheism and agrees with the Corinthian position: idols have no real existence.

verses.text: For though there are things that are called “gods”, whether in the heavens or on earth; as there are many “gods” and many “lords”;
verses.explainer: Even if there are so-called gods in heaven or on earth -- and indeed there are many 'gods' and many 'lords' -- Paul acknowledges that pagan culture is saturated with deities. He does not deny their cultural power, just their ontological reality.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Corinth's religious landscape", 'explanation': 'Archaeological excavations have uncovered temples to Aphrodite, Apollo, Asclepius, Demeter, Poseidon, and numerous other deities in Corinth. Temple dining rooms were the primary venues for social events -- business dinners, guild meetings, family celebrations. Avoiding them meant avoiding public life.'}

verses.text: yet to us there is one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and we for him; and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom are all things, and we live through him.
verses.explainer: Yet for us there is one God, the Father, from whom all things came, and one Lord, Jesus Christ, through whom all things exist. Paul adapts the Jewish Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4) and inserts Christ into it. This is one of the earliest and most explicit Christological statements in the New Testament.

verses.text: However, that knowledge isn’t in all men. But some, with consciousness of the idol until now, eat as of a thing sacrificed to an idol, and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
verses.explainer: But not everyone has this knowledge. Some, accustomed to idols until now, eat the food as really offered to an idol, and their conscience is defiled. The 'weak' believers -- likely recent converts from paganism -- cannot separate the meat from its pagan associations. For them, eating idol food feels like returning to idolatry.

thenVsNow.then: Former pagans who had spent years worshipping in these temples could not simply flip a mental switch and treat the meat as neutral. Their entire sensory and emotional experience of temple dining was bound up with idol worship.
thenVsNow.now: A modern parallel might be a recovering alcoholic who cannot be around alcohol, even if others can drink responsibly. The issue is not the substance itself but its power over a particular person's conscience and associations.

verses.text: But food will not commend us to God. For neither, if we don’t eat, are we the worse; nor, if we eat, are we the better.
verses.explainer: Food does not commend us to God. We are no worse off if we do not eat, and no better off if we do. Paul sides with the strong on the theological question: the food itself is morally neutral. But theological correctness does not end the discussion.

verses.text: But be careful that by no means does this liberty of yours become a stumbling block to the weak.
verses.explainer: But be careful that this right of yours does not become a stumbling block to the weak. Your freedom is real, but it carries responsibility. If exercising your right damages someone else's faith, you have misused your freedom.

verses.text: For if a man sees you who have knowledge sitting in an idol’s temple, won’t his conscience, if he is weak, be emboldened to eat things sacrificed to idols?
verses.explainer: If someone with a weak conscience sees you, with your knowledge, eating in an idol's temple, will they not be emboldened to eat food sacrificed to idols against their own conscience? Your example may pressure them into violating their own convictions.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Dining in idol temples', 'explanation': "Temple dining rooms in Corinth were like modern restaurants -- the social venue of choice. Invitations to dine 'at the table of Serapis' (a pagan deity) have been found on ancient papyri. Christians who continued dining in these spaces were participating in the social life of the city."}

verses.text: And through your knowledge, he who is weak perishes, the brother for whose sake Christ died.
verses.explainer: So the weak brother or sister, for whom Christ died, is destroyed by your knowledge. Paul raises the stakes dramatically: Christ died for this person. Your theological superiority is destroying someone Christ valued enough to die for.

verses.text: Thus, sinning against the brothers, and wounding their conscience when it is weak, you sin against Christ.
verses.explainer: When you sin against your brothers and sisters and wound their weak conscience, you sin against Christ. Hurting a fellow believer is not just a social faux pas; it is an offense against Christ himself.

verses.text: Therefore if food causes my brother to stumble, I will eat no meat forever more, that I don’t cause my brother to stumble.
verses.explainer: Therefore, if food causes my brother or sister to stumble, I will never eat meat again, so that I will not cause them to stumble. Paul makes the principle personal and absolute: he would permanently give up his freedom rather than damage someone else's faith. Love trumps liberty.

thenVsNow.then: In a culture where social dining was the primary means of networking and community, Paul's willingness to give up meat was a serious social sacrifice, not a minor inconvenience.
thenVsNow.now: The principle that love constrains liberty remains central to Christian ethics. The question is always: am I willing to limit my freedom for the sake of someone else's spiritual wellbeing?

explainer.summary: Paul illustrates the principle of chapter 8 (love limits freedom) with his own example. He has every right to receive financial support from the churches he serves -- the law, common sense, and Jesus himself all support this. But Paul has voluntarily given up this right so that no one can accuse him of preaching for profit. He compares himself to an athlete in training: disciplined, focused, willing to sacrifice anything for the prize. If Paul, an apostle, voluntarily limits his freedom, the Corinthians should be willing to do the same with their idol meat.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 9 is not a digression -- it is Paul's personal demonstration of the principle he just taught. Chapter 8 said love limits freedom; chapter 9 shows Paul living it out. He then transitions to chapter 10's warning about overconfidence.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The award given to the winner of an athletic compe
wordStudies.meaning: The award given to the winner of an athletic competition. In the Isthmian Games held near Corinth, the prize was a wreath of pine or celery. Paul contrasts this perishable crown with the imperishable crown that awaits faithful believers.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Rejected after testing, failing to meet the standa
wordStudies.meaning: Rejected after testing, failing to meet the standard. Used of metals that did not pass the assayer's test. Paul fears that after preaching to others, he himself could be found unfit -- not losing salvation per se, but being set aside from effective service.

verses.text: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Haven’t I seen Jesus Christ, our Lord? Aren’t you my work in the Lord?
verses.explainer: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord? Are you not my work in the Lord? Four rapid-fire rhetorical questions establishing Paul's credentials and freedom. If anyone has the right to exercise freedom, it is Paul.

verses.text: If to others I am not an apostle, yet at least I am to you; for you are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord.
verses.explainer: If Paul is not an apostle to others, he certainly is to the Corinthians. They are the seal of his apostleship in the Lord -- the living proof that his ministry is genuine. Their very existence as a church validates his calling.

verses.text: My defense to those who examine me is this.
verses.explainer: This is Paul's defense to those who would examine him. Some in Corinth are questioning his apostolic authority, possibly because he does not accept financial support like other apostles do.

verses.text: Have we no right to eat and to drink?
verses.explainer: Do we not have the right to eat and drink? Paul begins listing the rights he has chosen not to exercise. The right to food and drink at the church's expense is the most basic.

verses.text: Have we no right to take along a wife who is a believer, even as the rest of the apostles, and the brothers of the Lord, and Cephas?
verses.explainer: Do we not have the right to take along a believing wife, as do the other apostles and the brothers of the Lord and Cephas? This reveals that most apostles, including Peter and Jesus' own brothers, were married and traveled with their wives at the churches' expense.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "Apostles' wives", 'explanation': "This verse is significant evidence that Peter was married, that Jesus' brothers were active in church leadership, and that the early church financially supported not just ministers but their families. Paul's singleness was the exception, not the rule, among early church leaders."}

verses.text: Or have only Barnabas and I no right to not work?
verses.explainer: Or is it only Barnabas and I who have no right to refrain from working for a living? Paul and Barnabas are apparently the only major apostles who support themselves financially. Paul will explain why he made this unusual choice.

verses.text: What soldier ever serves at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard, and doesn’t eat of its fruit? Or who feeds a flock, and doesn’t drink from the flock’s milk?
verses.explainer: Three analogies from common life: Who serves as a soldier at his own expense? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat its grapes? Who shepherds a flock and does not drink the milk? In every profession, workers benefit from their labor.

verses.text: Do I speak these things according to the ways of men? Or doesn’t the law also say the same thing?
verses.explainer: Paul insists he is not just arguing from human logic. The law of Moses itself supports his point.

verses.text: For it is written in the law of Moses, “You shall not muzzle an ox while it treads out the grain.” Is it for the oxen that God cares,
verses.explainer: The law says: 'Do not muzzle an ox while it is treading out the grain.' Is God concerned only about oxen? Paul quotes Deuteronomy 25:4 and argues from lesser to greater: if God cares about the welfare of working animals, how much more about working ministers.

verses.text: or does he say it assuredly for our sake? Yes, it was written for our sake, because he who plows ought to plow in hope, and he who threshes in hope should partake of his hope.
verses.explainer: Surely this was written for us. The plowman should plow in hope, and the thresher should thresh in hope of sharing the harvest. Paul sees the Deuteronomy principle as applying broadly to all laborers, including gospel workers.

verses.text: If we sowed to you spiritual things, is it a great thing if we reap your fleshly things?
verses.explainer: If we have sown spiritual things among you, is it too much to reap material things from you? The exchange is fair: spiritual investment deserves material return.

verses.text: If others partake of this right over you, don’t we yet more? Nevertheless we did not use this right, but we bear all things, that we may cause no hindrance to the Good News of Christ.
verses.explainer: If others share this right over you, do we not even more? Yet Paul has not used this right. He endures everything rather than put an obstacle in the way of the gospel. Here is the thesis: Paul's refusal of support is not weakness but strategic self-denial for the gospel's sake.

thenVsNow.then: In the Greco-Roman world, accepting payment from students was how sophists (traveling teachers) operated. Some were accused of being mere hucksters. Paul's refusal to take money distinguished him from professional speakers and removed any suspicion of profit motive.
thenVsNow.now: The question of how churches should compensate their leaders remains fraught. Paul's example does not forbid paying ministers (he explicitly affirms the right) but establishes the principle that financial considerations must never compromise the gospel's credibility.

verses.text: Don’t you know that those who serve around sacred things eat from the things of the temple, and those who wait on the altar have their portion with the altar?
verses.explainer: Do you not know that those who serve in the temple get their food from the temple, and those who serve at the altar share in what is offered on the altar? Paul cites the Old Testament practice of priests being supported by the sacrificial system.

verses.text: Even so the Lord ordained that those who proclaim the Good News should live from the Good News.
verses.explainer: The Lord commanded that those who preach the gospel should live from the gospel. Paul cites Jesus' own instruction (Luke 10:7) that workers deserve their wages. The right to ministerial support has the highest possible endorsement.

verses.text: But I have used none of these things, and I don’t write these things that it may be done so in my case; for I would rather die, than that anyone should make my boasting void.
verses.explainer: But Paul has not used any of these rights. And he is not writing to claim them now. He would rather die than have anyone deprive him of his ground for boasting. His boast is that he preaches free of charge.

verses.text: For if I preach the Good News, I have nothing to boast about; for necessity is laid on me; but woe is to me, if I don’t preach the Good News.
verses.explainer: If Paul preaches the gospel, he has nothing to boast about -- he is compelled by divine necessity. Woe to him if he does not preach. Preaching is not Paul's career choice; it is his inescapable calling.

verses.text: For if I do this of my own will, I have a reward. But if not of my own will, I have a stewardship entrusted to me.
verses.explainer: If Paul does this willingly, he has a reward. If unwillingly, he is still entrusted with a stewardship. Either way, the obligation stands. Paul's freedom lies not in whether he preaches but in how he preaches -- specifically, free of charge.

verses.text: What then is my reward? That, when I preach the Good News, I may present the Good News of Christ without charge, so as not to abuse my authority in the Good News.
verses.explainer: What then is Paul's reward? That in preaching the gospel, he may present it free of charge and not make full use of his right. His reward is the act of surrender itself -- the satisfaction of offering the gospel as a pure gift with no strings attached.

verses.text: For though I was free from all, I brought myself under bondage to all, that I might gain the more.
verses.explainer: Though free from all, Paul has made himself a slave to all, to win as many as possible. This is the summary statement: maximum freedom voluntarily converted into maximum service.

thenVsNow.then: Voluntary slavery was incomprehensible in the Roman world. A free citizen choosing to act as a slave inverted every social value. Paul's self-description echoes Jesus' own teaching about the greatest being the servant of all.
thenVsNow.now: The concept of servant leadership -- power exercised through service rather than domination -- has entered mainstream management theory, but its origin is here: a free apostle choosing slavery for the sake of others.

verses.text: To the Jews I became as a Jew, that I might gain Jews; to those who are under the law, as under the law, that I might gain those who are under the law;
verses.explainer: To the Jews, Paul became as a Jew. To those under the law, as one under the law (though he himself is not under the law). Paul adapts his cultural behavior to his audience -- not to deceive but to remove unnecessary barriers to the gospel.

verses.text: to those who are without law, as without law (not being without law toward God, but under law toward Christ), that I might win those who are without law.
verses.explainer: To those outside the law, Paul became as one outside the law (not being outside God's law but under Christ's law). To the Gentiles, he drops Jewish cultural markers. He is not lawless but operates under a different law -- the law of Christ.

verses.text: To the weak I became as weak, that I might gain the weak. I have become all things to all men, that I may by all means save some.
verses.explainer: To the weak, Paul became weak. He has become all things to all people so that by all possible means he might save some. This is the most famous statement of Paul's missionary strategy: cultural flexibility in service of an unchanging message.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's willingness to cross cultural boundaries was controversial. Jewish Christians accused him of abandoning the Torah; Gentile Christians sometimes found his Jewish practices puzzling. Paul accepted criticism from both sides as the cost of reaching both.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of cultural adaptation in evangelism -- often called 'contextualization' -- remains both practiced and debated. How far can you adapt without compromising the message? Paul's example sets the pattern and the tension.

verses.text: Now I do this for the sake of the Good News, that I may be a joint partaker of it.
verses.explainer: Paul does all this for the sake of the gospel, so that he may share in its blessings. His adaptability is not people-pleasing but gospel-serving.

verses.text: Don’t you know that those who run in a race all run, but one receives the prize? Run like that, that you may win.
verses.explainer: Do you not know that in a race all the runners run, but only one receives the prize? Run in such a way as to win. The athletic metaphor would have hit home in Corinth -- the Isthmian Games, second only to the Olympics, were held just outside the city every two years.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Isthmian Games', 'explanation': 'The Isthmian Games were held at a sanctuary about ten miles from Corinth. They included foot races, wrestling, boxing, chariot racing, and musical competitions. Paul likely witnessed these games during his stay in Corinth. His athletic metaphors draw directly from this local context.'}

verses.text: Every man who strives in the games exercises self-control in all things. Now they do it to receive a corruptible crown, but we an incorruptible.
verses.explainer: Everyone who competes exercises self-control in all things. They do it for a perishable wreath, but we for an imperishable one. Isthmian athletes trained for months and won a pine wreath that would wilt within weeks. The Christian's prize is eternal.

verses.text: I therefore run like that, not aimlessly. I fight like that, not beating the air,
verses.explainer: Therefore Paul does not run aimlessly or box as one beating the air. His self-discipline has a target; his training is purposeful.

verses.text: but I beat my body and bring it into submission, lest by any means, after I have preached to others, I myself should be rejected.
verses.explainer: Paul disciplines his body and keeps it under control, so that after preaching to others he himself will not be disqualified. The apostle to the nations is not exempt from the standards he preaches. This shatters any illusion of spiritual complacency.

thenVsNow.then: In athletic competition, a disqualified athlete lost everything -- the prize, the honor, the years of training. The Greek word adokimos was used for coins that failed the assayer's test and were rejected as counterfeit.
thenVsNow.now: This verse challenges the idea that spiritual leaders are above accountability. If Paul worried about being disqualified, no Christian can afford complacency. The race requires ongoing discipline, not a one-time decision.

explainer.summary: Paul warns the overconfident Corinthians with a history lesson: Israel in the wilderness had every spiritual privilege -- baptism through the sea, spiritual food and drink, the presence of Christ -- yet most of them were destroyed because of idolatry, sexual immorality, and testing God. The Corinthians must not assume their sacraments (baptism, Lord's Supper) guarantee immunity from judgment. Paul then gives practical guidance on idol food: flee idolatry in the temple, but meat from the market is fine unless it troubles someone's conscience.
themes: Israel's wilderness failures as a warning
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 10 is the climax of the idol food discussion (chs. 8-10). After establishing the principle (ch. 8) and his personal example (ch. 9), Paul now adds the most serious argument: overconfidence leads to destruction, as Israel's history proves.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Patterns, prefigurations, examples
wordStudies.meaning: Patterns, prefigurations, examples. Paul reads Israel's wilderness history as a pattern that prefigures the Corinthian situation. The Israelites' experiences 'happened as examples for us.' The Old Testament is not just ancient history but a warning mirror for the church.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Testing, trial, temptation
wordStudies.meaning: Testing, trial, temptation. Can mean either God's testing (to prove character) or Satan's temptation (to destroy faith). Paul assures the Corinthians that no testing exceeds their capacity because God always provides an escape route.

verses.text: Now I would not have you ignorant, brothers, that our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea;
verses.explainer: Paul does not want them to be unaware: their ancestors were all under the cloud and all passed through the sea. The 'cloud' was God's guiding presence; the 'sea' was the Red Sea crossing. Israel had dramatic spiritual experiences -- yet that did not protect them.

verses.text: and were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea;
verses.explainer: They were all baptized into Moses in the cloud and in the sea. Paul draws a direct parallel between Israel's experience and Christian baptism. The Israelites were 'baptized' into their leader just as Christians are baptized into Christ.

verses.text: and all ate the same spiritual food;
verses.explainer: They all ate the same spiritual food. The manna from heaven was not just physical sustenance but a spiritual provision from God.

verses.text: and all drank the same spiritual drink. For they drank of a spiritual rock that followed them, and the rock was Christ.
verses.explainer: They all drank the same spiritual drink from a spiritual rock that followed them, and that rock was Christ. Paul identifies the source of Israel's water in the wilderness as the pre-incarnate Christ. There was a Jewish tradition that the rock from which Moses drew water traveled with Israel through the wilderness.

verses.text: However with most of them, God was not well pleased, for they were overthrown in the wilderness.
verses.explainer: But God was not pleased with most of them, and they were struck down in the wilderness. Despite all their spiritual privileges, the majority perished. The warning could not be clearer: sacramental experience does not guarantee spiritual security.

thenVsNow.then: According to Numbers 14:29-30, of the entire generation that left Egypt, only Joshua and Caleb entered the Promised Land. Everyone else died in the wilderness over forty years -- a massive rate of failure.
thenVsNow.now: This passage directly challenges any form of 'once saved, always saved' complacency. Paul's point is not that salvation is uncertain but that presumption is deadly. Spiritual privileges must be matched by faithful living.

verses.text: Now these things were our examples, to the intent we should not lust after evil things, as they also lusted.
verses.explainer: These things happened as examples (typoi) for us, so that we would not crave evil things as they did. The Old Testament is not just history; it is a warning mirror. Israel's failures are recorded for the church's benefit.

verses.text: Don’t be idolaters, as some of them were. As it is written, “The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play.”
verses.explainer: Do not be idolaters, as some of them were. Paul quotes Exodus 32:6: 'The people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play.' The golden calf incident involved a feast that descended into revelry -- the exact pattern Paul fears for Corinthians dining in idol temples.

verses.text: Let us not commit sexual immorality, as some of them committed, and in one day twenty-three thousand fell.
verses.explainer: We must not indulge in sexual immorality as some of them did, and twenty-three thousand fell in one day. Paul refers to the incident at Baal Peor, where Israelite men had sexual relations with Moabite women in the context of pagan worship.

verses.text: Let us not test Christ, as some of them tested, and perished by the serpents.
verses.explainer: We must not put Christ to the test as some of them did and were destroyed by serpents. Testing God means demanding proof of his care while simultaneously disobeying him.

verses.text: Don’t grumble, as some of them also grumbled, and perished by the destroyer.
verses.explainer: Do not grumble, as some of them did and were destroyed by the destroyer. Grumbling -- chronic dissatisfaction with God's provision and leadership -- was a recurring sin in the wilderness narrative.

verses.text: Now all these things happened to them by way of example, and they were written for our admonition, on whom the ends of the ages have come.
verses.explainer: These things happened to them as examples and were written down as warnings for us, on whom the ends of the ages have come. Paul sees the church as living at the climax of history -- the fulfillment of everything the Old Testament pointed toward.

verses.text: Therefore let him who thinks he stands be careful that he doesn’t fall.
verses.explainer: Therefore let anyone who thinks they stand take heed lest they fall. This is the direct application: the Corinthians' confidence in their spiritual status is exactly the kind of presumption that destroyed Israel in the wilderness.

verses.text: No temptation has taken you except what is common to man. God is faithful, who will not allow you to be tempted above what you are able, but will with the temptation also make the way of escape, that you may be able to endure it.
verses.explainer: No temptation has overtaken you except what is common to humanity. And God is faithful; he will not let you be tempted beyond what you can bear. But when you are tempted, he will also provide a way out so that you can endure it. One of the most quoted and most comforting promises in the New Testament.

thenVsNow.then: In a city saturated with pagan worship and sexual temptation, the Corinthians needed assurance that faithfulness was actually possible. Paul promises they are not facing anything unprecedented or insurmountable.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is widely cited in pastoral care and personal devotion. Its promise is not that temptation will be removed but that it will never exceed the believer's God-given capacity to resist. The 'way out' is escape, not annihilation of the temptation.

verses.text: Therefore, my beloved, flee from idolatry.
verses.explainer: Therefore, my beloved, flee from idolatry. After all the nuanced discussion about knowledge and freedom (ch. 8) and Israel's warnings (10:1-13), Paul's practical advice is blunt: run away. Do not test your limits; get out.

verses.text: I speak as to wise men. Judge what I say.
verses.explainer: I speak as to sensible people; judge for yourselves what I say. Paul appeals to their intellect -- the very thing they pride themselves on. Think this through logically.

verses.text: The cup of blessing which we bless, isn’t it a sharing of the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, isn’t it a sharing of the body of Christ?
verses.explainer: The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not a sharing in the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not a sharing in the body of Christ? Paul grounds his argument in the Lord's Supper: participating in the eucharistic meal creates a real communion (koinonia) with Christ.

verses.text: Because there is one loaf of bread, we, who are many, are one body; for we all partake of the one loaf of bread.
verses.explainer: Because there is one bread, we who are many are one body, for we all share the one bread. The shared meal creates shared identity. Eating together makes you one community.

verses.text: Consider Israel according to the flesh. Don’t those who eat the sacrifices participate in the altar?
verses.explainer: Consider the people of Israel: are not those who eat the sacrifices participants in the altar? In the Old Testament sacrificial system, eating the sacrifice created communion with God at the altar. The meal was not merely symbolic.

verses.text: What am I saying then? That a thing sacrificed to idols is anything, or that an idol is anything?
verses.explainer: Am I saying that food offered to an idol is anything, or that an idol is anything? No. Paul reaffirms his theological position: idols are nothing, and the food is nothing.

verses.text: But I say that the things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons, and not to God, and I don’t desire that you would have fellowship with demons.
verses.explainer: But what pagans sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons and not to God. Paul will not have them become participants with demons. The idols themselves are nothing, but the spiritual forces behind pagan worship are real and malevolent.

verses.text: You can’t both drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You can’t both partake of the table of the Lord, and of the table of demons.
verses.explainer: You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You cannot partake of the Lord's table and the table of demons. The exclusivity is absolute: the Lord's Supper and pagan temple feasts are mutually incompatible.

verses.text: Or do we provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than he?
verses.explainer: Shall we provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than he? Paul invokes the image of God as a jealous husband (from the Old Testament prophets) who will not share his people with rival deities.

verses.text: “All things are lawful for me,” but not all things are profitable. “All things are lawful for me,” but not all things build up.
verses.explainer: 'All things are lawful,' but not all things are beneficial. 'All things are lawful,' but not all things build up. Paul quotes the Corinthian slogan again (see 6:12) and again qualifies it: the test of freedom is whether it benefits others and builds up the community.

verses.text: Let no one seek his own, but each one his neighbor’s good.
verses.explainer: Let no one seek their own good but the good of their neighbor. This is the controlling principle: Christian decision-making is not primarily about my rights but about the impact on others.

verses.text: Whatever is sold in the butcher shop, eat, asking no question for the sake of conscience,
verses.explainer: Eat anything sold in the meat market without raising questions of conscience. Paul gives practical guidance: do not investigate the religious history of your groceries. Meat in the market is just meat.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The meat market (macellum)', 'explanation': "The macellum in Corinth was a central market where meat from various sources -- including leftover temple sacrifices -- was sold. There was no labeling system to distinguish 'idol meat' from regular meat. Paul says: do not ask, do not worry."}

verses.text: for “the earth is the Lord’s, and its fullness.”
verses.explainer: 'The earth is the Lord's and everything in it.' Paul quotes Psalm 24:1 as the theological basis for his freedom: all food belongs to God, regardless of what humans did with it in a pagan ceremony.

verses.text: But if one of those who don’t believe invites you to a meal, and you are inclined to go, eat whatever is set before you, asking no questions for the sake of conscience.
verses.explainer: If an unbeliever invites you to dinner and you want to go, eat whatever is set before you without raising questions. Paul allows full social engagement with non-Christians, including accepting dinner invitations where idol meat might be served.

verses.text: But if anyone says to you, “This was offered to idols,” don’t eat it for the sake of the one who told you, and for the sake of conscience. For “the earth is the Lord’s, and all its fullness.”
verses.explainer: But if someone says 'This was offered in sacrifice,' do not eat it -- for the sake of the one who told you and for the sake of conscience. If the information is volunteered, the situation changes. Now eating would send a signal that you endorse idol worship.

verses.text: Conscience, I say, not your own, but the other’s conscience. For why is my liberty judged by another conscience?
verses.explainer: I mean the other person's conscience, not your own. Why should my liberty be judged by another's conscience? Paul clarifies: the limitation is about the other person's understanding, not your own. Your own conscience is clear.

verses.text: If I partake with thankfulness, why am I denounced for something I give thanks for?
verses.explainer: If I partake with thankfulness, why am I denounced for that for which I give thanks? Paul acknowledges the tension: it feels unfair to limit your freedom because of someone else's scruples.

verses.text: Whether therefore you eat, or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.
verses.explainer: So whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do everything for the glory of God. This is the overarching principle that resolves all the specific cases. The question is not 'Is this permissible?' but 'Does this glorify God?'

thenVsNow.then: In a world where eating and drinking were deeply embedded in religious and social systems, Paul's principle offered a simple but demanding filter for every decision.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is one of the most quoted in Christian ethics. It transforms every mundane activity -- eating, working, socializing -- into an act of worship when done with the intention of honoring God.

verses.text: Give no occasion for stumbling, either to Jews, or to Greeks, or to the assembly of God;
verses.explainer: Give no offense to Jews or Greeks or to the church of God. Paul's concern extends to three audiences: the Jewish community, the pagan community, and the Christian community. Ideally, your behavior should not needlessly scandalize any of them.

verses.text: even as I also please all men in all things, not seeking my own profit, but the profit of the many, that they may be saved.
verses.explainer: Just as Paul tries to please everyone in everything -- not seeking his own advantage but the advantage of many, so that they may be saved. Paul returns to his own example (ch. 9): self-denial for the sake of others' salvation.

explainer.summary: Paul addresses two issues in worship. First, the question of head coverings: should women cover their heads when praying and prophesying? Paul argues yes, based on creation order and cultural propriety, though his reasoning is complex and his conclusion ('judge for yourselves') leaves some room for interpretation. Second, the Lord's Supper: the Corinthians have turned communion into a display of social inequality, with the wealthy eating lavishly while the poor go hungry. Paul is furious. He recounts the institution of the Lord's Supper and warns that eating and drinking 'in an unworthy manner' brings judgment.
themes: Head coverings and gender in worship
explainer.contextInBook: Paul shifts from questions about food (chs. 8-10) to questions about worship practices. Both the head covering issue and the Lord's Supper abuse reflect the same root problem: the Corinthians are importing their culture's status hierarchies into the church.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Head, either literally (the top of the body) or me
wordStudies.meaning: Head, either literally (the top of the body) or metaphorically. The metaphorical meaning is debated: some scholars read it as 'authority over,' others as 'source/origin.' This ambiguity affects the interpretation of the entire head-covering passage.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Recognizing, distinguishing, properly evaluating t
wordStudies.meaning: Recognizing, distinguishing, properly evaluating the body. At the Lord's Supper, this likely means recognizing the community of believers as the body of Christ. Failing to 'discern the body' means ignoring the poor and marginalized members while stuffing yourself.

verses.text: Be imitators of me, even as I also am of Christ.
verses.explainer: Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ. This verse logically belongs to the end of chapter 10, concluding Paul's example of self-denial. He dares to say 'imitate me' only because his own pattern imitates Christ's.

verses.text: Now I praise you, brothers, that you remember me in all things, and hold firm the traditions, even as I delivered them to you.
verses.explainer: Paul praises them for remembering him and maintaining the traditions he delivered to them. A rare positive note before the criticism begins.

verses.text: But I would have you know that the head of every man is Christ, and the head of the woman is man, and the head of Christ is God.
verses.explainer: But Paul wants them to understand that the head of every man is Christ, the head of woman is man, and the head of Christ is God. This hierarchical statement is the foundation for the head-covering argument, though the meaning of 'head' (kephale) is intensely debated.

thenVsNow.then: In the Greco-Roman world, social hierarchy was the assumed framework for all relationships. Paul's hierarchy (God-Christ-man-woman) would have been less controversial in its original context than it is today.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to debates about gender roles in the church. Complementarians read 'head' as 'authority,' supporting male leadership. Egalitarians read 'head' as 'source' or 'origin,' with no implication of authority. The debate continues.

verses.text: Every man praying or prophesying, having his head covered, dishonors his head.
verses.explainer: Every man who prays or prophesies with his head covered dishonors his head. In Roman worship, men typically covered their heads. Paul may be asking Christian men to break with Roman convention and worship with uncovered heads, distinguishing Christian worship from pagan.

verses.text: But every woman praying or prophesying with her head uncovered dishonors her head. For it is one and the same thing as if she were shaved.
verses.explainer: But every woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered dishonors her head. Notably, Paul assumes women pray and prophesy in public worship -- the question is not whether they participate but how they should dress while doing so.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Women prophesying', 'explanation': "Paul does not question women's right to pray and prophesy publicly. This is significant: he assumes women have an active vocal role in worship. The apparent tension with 14:34 ('women should be silent') has generated enormous scholarly debate."}

verses.text: For if a woman is not covered, let her hair also be cut off. But if it is shameful for a woman to have her hair cut off or be shaved, let her be covered.
verses.explainer: If a woman does not cover her head, she might as well have her hair cut off. And if it is shameful for a woman to have her hair cut or shaved off, she should cover her head. Paul's argument links head covering to cultural standards of honor and shame.

verses.text: For a man indeed ought not to have his head covered, because he is the image and glory of God, but the woman is the glory of the man.
verses.explainer: A man should not cover his head, since he is the image and glory of God; but the woman is the glory of man. Paul's reasoning draws on the creation narrative in Genesis 2.

verses.text: For man is not from woman, but woman from man;
verses.explainer: For man did not come from woman, but woman from man. Paul references the creation of Eve from Adam's side in Genesis 2.

verses.text: for man wasn’t created for the woman, but woman for the man.
verses.explainer: Neither was man created for woman, but woman for man. Paul continues the Genesis 2 argument about the order and purpose of creation.

verses.text: For this cause the woman ought to have authority over her own head, because of the angels.
verses.explainer: For this reason, a woman should have authority on her head, because of the angels. One of the most enigmatic verses in Paul's letters. The 'authority on her head' may be her own authority to pray and prophesy (the head covering being a sign of her authorized participation) or it may refer to authority in the hierarchical sense.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Because of the angels', 'explanation': "The reference to angels is mysterious. Possible explanations: angels attend worship and would be offended by impropriety; angels who 'fell' through sexual desire (Genesis 6:1-4) could be tempted by uncovered women; or angels as guardians of proper order in worship. No interpretation is universally accepted."}

verses.text: Nevertheless, neither is the woman independent of the man, nor the man independent of the woman, in the Lord.
verses.explainer: Nevertheless, in the Lord woman is not independent of man, nor man independent of woman. Paul immediately qualifies the hierarchy with mutuality. In Christ, neither gender exists independently of the other.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's qualification was remarkable in a patriarchal culture. By insisting on mutual dependence, he undermined the very hierarchy he had just established. This tension may be intentional -- Paul is navigating between cultural norms and gospel equality.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is crucial for egalitarian readings of Paul. Even within the head-covering argument, Paul insists on mutual dependence and equal necessity. Neither gender is complete without the other.

verses.text: For as woman came from man, so a man also comes through a woman; but all things are from God.
verses.explainer: For just as woman came from man, so man comes through woman -- and all things come from God. The creation order (woman from man) is balanced by the natural order (every man is born from a woman). God is the ultimate source of both.

verses.text: Judge for yourselves. Is it appropriate that a woman pray to God unveiled?
verses.explainer: Judge for yourselves: is it proper for a woman to pray to God with her head uncovered? Paul appeals to their own judgment -- a surprisingly open-ended conclusion for what seemed like a firm directive.

verses.text: Doesn’t even nature itself teach you that if a man has long hair, it is a dishonor to him?
verses.explainer: Does not nature itself teach you that if a man has long hair, it is a disgrace to him? Paul appeals to 'nature,' but hair length conventions vary enormously across cultures. This suggests Paul is appealing to local Corinthian norms rather than universal principles.

verses.text: But if a woman has long hair, it is a glory to her, for her hair is given to her for a covering.
verses.explainer: But if a woman has long hair, it is her glory. For long hair is given to her as a covering. This complicates the argument further: if a woman's long hair already serves as a covering, why does she need an additional covering? Some scholars think Paul is actually concluding that hair is the only covering needed.

verses.text: But if any man seems to be contentious, we have no such custom, neither do God’s assemblies.
verses.explainer: If anyone is inclined to be contentious, we have no such practice, nor do the churches of God. Paul's final word: this is what the churches practice. He does not invite further debate. The appeal to universal church practice is his closing argument.

verses.text: But in giving you this command, I don’t praise you, that you come together not for the better but for the worse.
verses.explainer: Paul now shifts to the Lord's Supper, and his tone changes from complex reasoning to sharp rebuke. Their gatherings do more harm than good. This is a devastating indictment.

verses.text: For first of all, when you come together in the assembly, I hear that divisions exist among you, and I partly believe it.
verses.explainer: Paul hears there are divisions when they assemble as a church, and he partly believes it. The factionalism from chapters 1-4 is playing out in their worship services.

verses.text: For there also must be factions among you, that those who are approved may be revealed among you.
verses.explainer: There must be factions among you so that those who are genuine may be recognized. Paul sees the factions as serving a grim purpose: they reveal who is truly committed to Christ and who is just pursuing social advantage.

verses.text: When therefore you assemble yourselves together, it is not the Lord’s supper that you eat.
verses.explainer: When they come together, it is not really to eat the Lord's Supper. The meal they share has been so corrupted that it no longer deserves the name.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': "The Lord's Supper as a meal", 'explanation': "In the early church, the Lord's Supper was not a symbolic nibble of bread and sip of wine but a full communal meal (a 'love feast'). Wealthy members brought abundant food; poor members brought little or nothing. The meal was supposed to equalize everyone at Christ's table -- but in Corinth, the opposite was happening."}

verses.text: For in your eating each one takes his own supper first. One is hungry, and another is drunken.
verses.explainer: Each one goes ahead with their own meal. One goes hungry while another gets drunk. The wealthy patrons are eating and drinking lavishly before the working-class members can even arrive (slaves and laborers had less control over their schedules).

thenVsNow.then: Roman dinner parties were rigidly stratified: the host and honored guests received better food and wine than lower-status guests. The Corinthians were reproducing this social hierarchy at the Lord's table.
thenVsNow.now: Any church practice that creates a two-tier system -- where the wealthy or powerful are treated differently from the poor or marginalized -- is committing the Corinthian error. Paul's critique applies to any form of social stratification within the worshipping community.

verses.text: What, don’t you have houses to eat and to drink in? Or do you despise God’s assembly, and put them to shame who don’t have enough? What shall I tell you? Shall I praise you? In this I don’t praise you.
verses.explainer: Do you not have homes to eat and drink in? Or do you despise the church of God and humiliate those who have nothing? Paul is indignant. The Lord's Supper is meant to be the great equalizer, and they have turned it into a display of privilege.

verses.text: For I received from the Lord that which also I delivered to you, that the Lord Jesus on the night in which he was betrayed took bread.
verses.explainer: Paul passes on what he received from the Lord: on the night the Lord Jesus was betrayed, he took bread. This is the earliest written account of the institution of the Lord's Supper -- written before any of the Gospels.

verses.text:
When he had given thanks, he broke it, and said,
“Take, eat. This is my body, which is broken for you. Do this in memory of me.”
verses.explainer: He gave thanks, broke it, and said 'This is my body, which is for you. Do this in remembrance of me.' The words of institution that have been repeated billions of times in Christian worship for two thousand years.

verses.text:
In the same way he also took the cup, after supper, saying,
“This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink, in memory of me.”
verses.explainer: In the same way, after supper he took the cup and said 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, whenever you drink it, in remembrance of me.' The 'new covenant' language points back to Jeremiah 31:31-34 -- God's promise of a new relationship with his people.

verses.text: For as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes.
verses.explainer: Whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes. The Lord's Supper is simultaneously a look back (remembering Christ's death) and a look forward (anticipating his return). It bridges past and future in the present moment.

verses.text: Therefore whoever eats this bread or drinks the Lord’s cup in a way unworthy of the Lord will be guilty of the body and the blood of the Lord.
verses.explainer: Whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord. 'Unworthy manner' in this context means eating while ignoring the poor and maintaining social divisions. The sin is not personal unworthiness but communal indifference.

verses.text: But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of the bread, and drink of the cup.
verses.explainer: Let a person examine themselves, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup. Self-examination is required, but the point is not introspective guilt -- it is checking whether you are treating the community as Christ's body.

verses.text: For he who eats and drinks in an unworthy way eats and drinks judgment to himself, if he doesn’t discern the Lord’s body.
verses.explainer: For anyone who eats and drinks without discerning the body eats and drinks judgment on themselves. 'Discerning the body' means recognizing the community of believers as Christ's body and treating every member -- especially the poorest -- with the dignity that deserves.

verses.text: For this cause many among you are weak and sickly, and not a few sleep.
verses.explainer: That is why many among you are weak and sick, and some have died. Paul draws a direct causal connection between their abuse of the Lord's Supper and illness and death in the community. This is one of the most sobering statements in the New Testament.

verses.text: For if we discerned ourselves, we wouldn’t be judged.
verses.explainer: If we judged ourselves, we would not come under judgment. Self-correction prevents divine correction.

verses.text: But when we are judged, we are punished by the Lord, that we may not be condemned with the world.
verses.explainer: But when we are judged by the Lord, we are being disciplined so that we will not be condemned with the world. Even God's judgment on the Corinthians is remedial, not final. It is discipline to prevent ultimate condemnation.

verses.text: Therefore, my brothers, when you come together to eat, wait for one another.
verses.explainer: Therefore, when you come together to eat, wait for one another. The practical solution is simple: wait until everyone is present before eating. Do not let the wealthy finish before the poor arrive.

verses.text: But if anyone is hungry, let him eat at home, lest your coming together be for judgment. The rest I will set in order whenever I come.
verses.explainer: If anyone is hungry, let them eat at home, so that your coming together will not result in judgment. If you cannot wait, eat at home and come to the Lord's Supper ready to share equally.

explainer.summary: Paul turns to the question of spiritual gifts (pneumatika), which the Corinthians have been using as a competitive ranking system. He establishes three foundational principles: all genuine gifts come from the same Spirit, God distributes gifts as he chooses (no one can claim superiority), and the church is like a human body -- diverse parts working together, each essential and none expendable. The famous body of Christ metaphor (12:12-27) is Paul's most sustained argument for church unity amid diversity.
themes: Diversity of gifts from one Spirit
God's preferential honor for the 'weaker' members
explainer.contextInBook: This begins the second 'now concerning' section on spiritual gifts (chs. 12-14). The Corinthians were ranking gifts hierarchically, with tongues at the top. Paul will spend three chapters dismantling this hierarchy: ch. 12 establishes unity in diversity, ch. 13 elevates love above all gifts, and ch. 14 gives practical guidelines.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Paul uses two words: pneumatika (spiritual things/
wordStudies.meaning: Paul uses two words: pneumatika (spiritual things/manifestations) and charismata (grace-gifts). The first emphasizes the Spirit as source; the second emphasizes that they are unearned gifts, not achievements. Both terms undercut the Corinthian tendency to treat gifts as personal accomplishments.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The physical body, used metaphorically for the com
wordStudies.meaning: The physical body, used metaphorically for the community of believers. The body metaphor was common in Greco-Roman political thought (the state as a body) but Paul radically democratizes it: in the political version, the 'head' rules; in Paul's version, the 'weaker' parts receive greater honor.

verses.text: Now concerning spiritual things, brothers, I don’t want you to be ignorant.
verses.explainer: Now concerning spiritual gifts, brothers and sisters, Paul does not want them to be uninformed. The 'now concerning' formula signals another question from their letter.

verses.text: You know that when you were heathen, you were led away to those mute idols, however you might be led.
verses.explainer: When they were pagans, they were led astray to mute idols, however they were led. The Corinthians' pagan background included ecstatic religious experiences -- being 'carried away' by spiritual forces. Paul needs to help them distinguish genuine Spirit-led manifestations from pagan ecstasy.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Pagan ecstatic worship', 'explanation': 'Corinthian paganism included ecstatic practices: the Dionysian cult involved frenzied dancing and intoxication; the oracle at Delphi involved a priestess who delivered messages in a trance state. The Corinthians may have confused Christian spiritual gifts with these familiar pagan phenomena.'}

verses.text: Therefore I make known to you that no man speaking by God’s Spirit says, “Jesus is accursed.” No one can say, “Jesus is Lord,” but by the Holy Spirit.
verses.explainer: No one speaking by the Spirit of God says 'Jesus is cursed,' and no one can say 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit. Paul provides a basic test: the Spirit always leads toward confessing Jesus as Lord, never toward cursing him.

verses.text: Now there are various kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit.
verses.explainer: There are different kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit. Diversity of gifts does not mean diversity of source. One Spirit produces a wide variety of manifestations.

verses.text: There are various kinds of service, and the same Lord.
verses.explainer: There are different kinds of service, but the same Lord. Diversity of ministries flows from one Lord who assigns and empowers them all.

verses.text: There are various kinds of workings, but the same God, who works all things in all.
verses.explainer: There are different kinds of working, but the same God works all of them in all people. The Trinitarian structure is implicit: one Spirit, one Lord, one God -- the source of all diversity in the church.

verses.text: But to each one is given the manifestation of the Spirit for the profit of all.
verses.explainer: To each person the manifestation of the Spirit is given for the common good. Two critical points: every believer has a gift (not just the elite), and every gift is for the community's benefit, not the individual's glory.

verses.text: For to one is given through the Spirit the word of wisdom, and to another the word of knowledge, according to the same Spirit;
verses.explainer: To one is given the word of wisdom through the Spirit, to another the word of knowledge. Paul begins listing gifts, though this list is not exhaustive -- other lists in Romans 12, Ephesians 4, and 1 Peter 4 include different gifts.

verses.text: to another faith, by the same Spirit; and to another gifts of healings, by the same Spirit;
verses.explainer: To another faith by the same Spirit, to another gifts of healing by the one Spirit. 'Faith' here is not saving faith (which all Christians have) but a special charismatic gift of extraordinary trust in God for specific situations.

verses.text: and to another workings of miracles; and to another prophecy; and to another discerning of spirits; to another different kinds of languages; and to another the interpretation of languages.
verses.explainer: To another the working of miracles, to another prophecy, to another the discernment of spirits, to another different kinds of tongues, and to another the interpretation of tongues. Paul lists nine gifts, but this is a sample, not a complete catalog.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Tongues (glossolalia)', 'explanation': 'Speaking in tongues was the gift the Corinthians prized most -- ecstatic speech in unknown languages. Paul will spend all of chapter 14 arguing that prophecy is more beneficial to the community than tongues, because prophecy communicates intelligible content.'}

verses.text: But the one and the same Spirit produces all of these, distributing to each one separately as he desires.
verses.explainer: All these are the work of one and the same Spirit, who distributes them to each one individually as he wills. The Spirit is sovereign in gift distribution. You do not earn or choose your gift; the Spirit assigns it. This demolishes the gift hierarchy.

verses.text: For as the body is one, and has many members, and all the members of the body, being many, are one body; so also is Christ.
verses.explainer: For just as the body is one and has many parts, and all the parts of the body, though many, are one body, so it is with Christ. Paul introduces the body metaphor that will dominate the rest of the chapter. The church is not like a body; it is Christ's body.

thenVsNow.then: The body metaphor was familiar in Greco-Roman political philosophy. Philosophers used it to argue that each social class should stay in its place. Paul radically revises the metaphor: in his version, the 'less honorable' parts receive greater honor.
