thenVsNow.now: The body of Christ metaphor remains the most widely used image for the church in Christian theology and practice. It affirms both diversity (many parts) and unity (one body) as essential features of the church.

verses.text: For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body, whether Jews or Greeks, whether bond or free; and were all given to drink into one Spirit.
verses.explainer: For in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body -- whether Jews or Greeks, slaves or free -- and we were all given one Spirit to drink. Baptism is the great equalizer: it erases the social categories that divide the ancient world.

verses.text: For the body is not one member, but many.
verses.explainer: The body is not one part but many. Simple but fundamental: uniformity is not the goal. A body made entirely of one organ would not be a body at all.

verses.text: If the foot would say, “Because I’m not the hand, I’m not part of the body,” it is not therefore not part of the body.
verses.explainer: If the foot says 'Because I am not a hand, I do not belong to the body,' that does not make it any less a part of the body. Self-exclusion based on feeling inferior or different does not change the reality of belonging.

verses.text: If the ear would say, “Because I’m not the eye, I’m not part of the body,” it’s not therefore not part of the body.
verses.explainer: If the ear says 'Because I am not an eye, I do not belong to the body,' that does not make it any less a part. Paul repeats the point with a second example. Feeling inadequate does not remove you from the body.

verses.text: If the whole body were an eye, where would the hearing be? If the whole were hearing, where would the smelling be?
verses.explainer: If the whole body were an eye, where would the hearing be? If the whole body were an ear, where would the sense of smell be? The absurdity argument: a body consisting of a single organ is not a body. Diversity is not a problem to be solved but a design feature.

verses.text: But now God has set the members, each one of them, in the body, just as he desired.
verses.explainer: But in fact God has placed the parts in the body, every one of them, just as he wanted them to be. God deliberately designed the body with diverse parts. If you have a particular gift, God put you there. If your gift seems less glamorous, God chose that too.

thenVsNow.then: In a competitive culture where everyone wanted to be at the top, Paul insists that every position in the body is God's deliberate placement. Your role is not an accident or a consolation prize.
thenVsNow.now: In churches where platform ministry (preaching, worship leading) receives disproportionate attention, Paul's insistence that God placed every member in the body challenges the celebrity culture that elevates visible gifts over invisible ones.

verses.text: If they were all one member, where would the body be?
verses.explainer: If they were all one part, where would the body be? The single-organ absurdity again. Uniformity destroys the body rather than strengthening it.

verses.text: But now they are many members, but one body.
verses.explainer: As it is, there are many parts but one body. Paul states the thesis plainly: many parts, one body. Both diversity and unity are essential.

verses.text: The eye can’t tell the hand, “I have no need for you,” or again the head to the feet, “I have no need for you.”
verses.explainer: The eye cannot say to the hand 'I do not need you.' The head cannot say to the feet 'I do not need you.' This addresses the opposite problem from verses 15-16: not self-exclusion by the 'lesser' parts but exclusion of others by the 'greater' parts. The gifted members cannot dismiss the less visible members.

verses.text: No, much rather, those members of the body which seem to be weaker are necessary.
verses.explainer: On the contrary, the parts of the body that seem weaker are indispensable. Internal organs (heart, liver, lungs) are invisible and seem less impressive than hands or eyes, but without them the body dies. Paul radically inverts the status hierarchy.

verses.text: Those parts of the body which we think to be less honorable, on those we bestow more abundant honor; and our unpresentable parts have more abundant propriety;
verses.explainer: The parts we think are less honorable we treat with special honor, and our unpresentable parts are treated with special modesty. We clothe and protect the 'dishonorable' parts of the body more carefully than the 'honorable' ones.

thenVsNow.then: In Roman society, honor was a zero-sum game: more honor for one person meant less for another. Paul inverts this: the less honored receive more honor. God's economy runs opposite to the world's.
thenVsNow.now: This principle challenges every community to ask: are we giving extra honor to our most vulnerable and least visible members? Or do we lavish attention on the already prominent?

verses.text: whereas our presentable parts have no such need. But God composed the body together, giving more abundant honor to the inferior part,
verses.explainer: Our presentable parts need no special treatment. But God has combined the members of the body and has given greater honor to the parts that lacked it.

verses.text: that there should be no division in the body, but that the members should have the same care for one another.
verses.explainer: The purpose: so that there should be no division in the body, but that its parts should have equal concern for each other. God's design eliminates competition. Mutual care, not hierarchical ranking, is the body's operating principle.

verses.text: When one member suffers, all the members suffer with it. Or when one member is honored, all the members rejoice with it.
verses.explainer: If one part suffers, every part suffers with it; if one part is honored, every part rejoices with it. The interconnection is total. There is no isolated suffering or private glory in the body of Christ. This is the basis for Christian empathy and solidarity.

verses.text: Now you are the body of Christ, and members individually.
verses.explainer: Now you are the body of Christ, and individually members of it. Paul makes the application explicit: this is not an abstract metaphor but your actual identity. You, the Corinthian church, are Christ's body.

verses.text: God has set some in the assembly: first apostles, second prophets, third teachers, then miracle workers, then gifts of healings, helps, governments, and various kinds of languages.
verses.explainer: God has appointed in the church first apostles, second prophets, third teachers, then miracles, then gifts of healing, helping, administrating, and various kinds of tongues. Paul lists a hierarchy of function (note the numbering: first, second, third) but puts tongues -- the Corinthians' favorite -- dead last.

verses.text: Are all apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Are all miracle workers?
verses.explainer: Are all apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Do all work miracles? The expected answer to every question is no. The gifts are distributed, not concentrated. No one has all of them.

verses.text: Do all have gifts of healings? Do all speak with various languages? Do all interpret?
verses.explainer: Do all have gifts of healing? Do all speak in tongues? Do all interpret? Again, no. The rhetorical questions demolish the idea that one particular gift (especially tongues) is the mark of spiritual maturity.

verses.text: But earnestly desire the best gifts. Moreover, I show a most excellent way to you.
verses.explainer: But eagerly desire the greater gifts. And yet I will show you the most excellent way. Paul redirects their ambition: desire the gifts that build up the community most (prophecy over tongues). But above all gifts, there is something greater -- and that is the subject of the next chapter.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthians were eager for the most spectacular gifts. Paul channels that eagerness toward the most constructive gifts and then transcends the entire gift discussion by pointing to love.
thenVsNow.now: Chapter 12 ends on a cliffhanger that introduces the love chapter. The 'most excellent way' is not a gift at all but the disposition that must govern every gift: love. Without it, even the greatest gifts are worthless.

explainer.summary: Chapter 13 is the most famous passage in 1 Corinthians and one of the most beloved texts in the Bible. But it is not a freestanding poem about romantic love -- it is the centerpiece of Paul's argument about spiritual gifts. Sandwiched between chapter 12 (the diversity of gifts) and chapter 14 (the regulation of gifts), chapter 13 insists that without love, even the most spectacular gifts are empty noise. Paul defines love not as a feeling but as a set of behaviors: patience, kindness, humility, endurance. He then argues that love outlasts every spiritual gift -- prophecy, tongues, and knowledge will all cease, but love endures forever. The chapter climaxes with the famous triad: faith, hope, and love remain, and the greatest is love.
themes: Love as the supreme virtue governing all gifts
Love defined by actions not emotions
The temporary nature of spiritual gifts
Faith hope and love -- the abiding triad
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 13 is not a digression or a poetic interlude inserted randomly. It is the theological hinge of the spiritual gifts argument (chs. 12-14). Chapter 12 ended by promising to show 'the most excellent way.' Chapter 13 reveals that way: love. Without love, the entire gifts discussion is pointless. Chapter 14 will then apply the love principle to specific worship practices.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The distinctive New Testament word for love, chose
wordStudies.meaning: The distinctive New Testament word for love, chosen deliberately over eros (romantic/sexual love) and philia (friendship/affection). Agape is self-giving love that seeks the good of the other regardless of the other's worthiness or response. It is not primarily an emotion but a disposition of the will -- a commitment to act for someone's benefit. In the Septuagint (Greek Old Testament), agape translates the Hebrew hesed (covenant faithfulness). Paul's use here grounds love not in human sentiment but in the character of God.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To know fully, to recognize completely
wordStudies.meaning: To know fully, to recognize completely. The prefix epi intensifies the root verb ginosko (to know). Paul says that now we know partially, but then we will know fully 'as we are fully known.' The passive voice is crucial: we are already fully known by God. Our future knowledge will match the knowledge God already has of us.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A mirror, typically made of polished bronze in the
wordStudies.meaning: A mirror, typically made of polished bronze in the ancient world. Corinth was famous for manufacturing bronze mirrors, but even the best ancient mirrors produced dim, imperfect reflections compared to seeing someone face to face. Paul uses this familiar Corinthian product to illustrate the partial nature of present spiritual knowledge.

verses.text: If I speak with the languages of men and of angels, but don’t have love, I have become sounding brass, or a clanging cymbal.
verses.explainer: If I speak in the tongues of men and of angels but have not love, I am a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal. Paul begins with the gift the Corinthians valued most -- tongues -- and declares it worthless without love. The 'noisy gong' metaphor is drawn from pagan worship: bronze instruments were used in the cult of Cybele and Dionysus to create ecstatic noise. Without love, tongues-speaking is indistinguishable from pagan cacophony.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Noisy gong', 'explanation': "Bronze percussion instruments were prominent in Corinthian pagan worship, especially in the ecstatic cults of Cybele and Dionysus. The worshippers would clang cymbals and gongs to induce trance states. Paul's image would have been immediately recognizable: tongues without love sounds like paganism, not Christianity."}

verses.text: If I have the gift of prophecy, and know all mysteries and all knowledge; and if I have all faith, so as to remove mountains, but don’t have love, I am nothing.
verses.explainer: If I have the gift of prophecy and can fathom all mysteries and all knowledge, and if I have a faith that can move mountains, but have not love, I am nothing. Paul escalates from tongues to the gifts he himself values most highly -- prophecy, knowledge, faith -- and declares even these worthless without love. A person with extraordinary spiritual power but no love is not merely ineffective; they are 'nothing.'

verses.text: If I give away all my goods to feed the poor, and if I give my body to be burned, but don’t have love, it profits me nothing.
verses.explainer: If I give all I possess to the poor and surrender my body to the flames, but have not love, I gain nothing. Paul reaches the extreme: total self-sacrifice, giving away every possession and even one's own life. Without love, even martyrdom is meaningless. This demolishes any merit-based understanding of sacrifice -- the motive matters as much as the act.

thenVsNow.then: In the ancient world, dramatic acts of self-sacrifice (including voluntary death) were honored as the highest virtue. Philosophers celebrated those who died for their convictions. Paul shockingly declares that even the ultimate sacrifice, without love, earns nothing.
thenVsNow.now: This verse challenges every form of performative religion -- giving, serving, sacrificing not out of love but out of guilt, duty, or the desire to be admired. Paul insists that the inner disposition transforms the outward act.

verses.text: Love is patient and is kind; love doesn’t envy. Love doesn’t brag, is not proud,
verses.explainer: Love is patient, love is kind. It does not envy, it does not boast, it is not proud. Paul now defines love through fifteen verbs -- not abstract nouns. Love is what love does. Patience and kindness are the positive foundation; envy, boasting, and pride are the first negatives. Each of these vices was a specific problem in Corinth: the factions boasted about their leaders, the gift-holders were puffed up, and the wealthy envied each other's status.

verses.text: doesn’t behave itself inappropriately, doesn’t seek its own way, is not provoked, takes no account of evil;
verses.explainer: It is not rude, it is not self-seeking, it is not easily angered, it keeps no record of wrongs. Four more negatives. 'Not self-seeking' strikes at the heart of the Corinthian problem: they were exercising their rights without regard for others. 'Keeps no record of wrongs' uses an accounting term (logizomai) -- love does not maintain a ledger of offenses.

verses.text: doesn’t rejoice in unrighteousness, but rejoices with the truth;
verses.explainer: Love does not delight in evil but rejoices with the truth. Love finds no pleasure in another's moral failure. In a community riddled with factions, the temptation to celebrate a rival's downfall was real. Love resists that impulse and instead finds joy when truth prevails.

verses.text: bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things.
verses.explainer: It always protects, always trusts, always hopes, always perseveres. Four 'always' statements that close the definition. Love's endurance is absolute and unconditional. The Greek verb stegei (protects/bears) can mean to cover, to shield from exposure, or to bear up under weight. Love covers the faults of others rather than exposing them.

thenVsNow.then: In the honor-shame culture of Corinth, exposing someone's faults was a weapon. Public shame could destroy a person's social standing. Love refuses to wield that weapon.
thenVsNow.now: In an age of social media and public shaming, 'love covers' remains a radical countercultural principle. The instinct to expose, cancel, or humiliate is the opposite of what Paul describes here.

verses.text: Love never fails. But where there are prophecies, they will be done away with. Where there are various languages, they will cease. Where there is knowledge, it will be done away with.
verses.explainer: Love never fails. But where there are prophecies, they will cease; where there are tongues, they will be stilled; where there is knowledge, it will pass away. Paul pivots from defining love to arguing for its permanence. Every spiritual gift the Corinthians prize is temporary. Prophecy, tongues, and knowledge all have an expiration date. Love alone endures forever.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthians were building their identities and hierarchies around spiritual gifts -- especially tongues. Paul tells them they are investing in depreciating assets. Only love holds its value.
thenVsNow.now: The cessationist-continuationist debate often centers on this verse: when do prophecy and tongues 'cease'? Cessationists say they ended with the apostolic age. Continuationists say they continue until Christ's return. Paul's main point, however, is not about when gifts end but about what outlasts them: love.

verses.text: For we know in part, and we prophesy in part;
verses.explainer: For we know in part and we prophesy in part. Present spiritual knowledge, however impressive, is fragmentary. Even genuine prophecy captures only a portion of divine truth.

verses.text: but when that which is complete has come, then that which is partial will be done away with.
verses.explainer: But when completeness comes, what is in part disappears. The 'completeness' (Greek to teleion -- the perfect, the complete, the mature) likely refers to the eschatological fulfillment when Christ returns and we see God face to face. Present gifts are scaffolding; they will be removed when the building is complete.

verses.text: When I was a child, I spoke as a child, I felt as a child, I thought as a child. Now that I have become a man, I have put away childish things.
verses.explainer: When I was a child, I talked like a child, I thought like a child, I reasoned like a child. When I became an adult, I put the ways of childhood behind me. Paul uses the childhood-to-adulthood analogy: spiritual gifts in this age are like a child's way of understanding -- real but immature compared to what is coming. The Corinthians, who pride themselves on spiritual maturity, are actually in the childhood stage.

verses.text: For now we see in a mirror, dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part, but then I will know fully, even as I was also fully known.
verses.explainer: For now we see only a reflection as in a mirror; then we shall see face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I am fully known. One of the most profound verses in the New Testament. Corinth was famous for producing bronze mirrors, but even the best gave a dim, distorted reflection. Paul says all present spiritual experience -- visions, prophecy, tongues, knowledge -- is like looking at a bronze mirror. The reality it reflects is real, but the image is imperfect. One day we will see clearly, face to face, with no mediating surface.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Corinthian mirrors', 'explanation': "Corinth was renowned throughout the ancient world for its bronze work, especially mirrors. Pliny, Pausanias, and other ancient writers mention Corinthian bronze as a luxury product. Paul's audience would have immediately understood the metaphor: they looked into Corinthian mirrors every day and knew the image was real but imperfect."}

verses.text: But now faith, hope, and love remain—these three. The greatest of these is love.
verses.explainer: And now these three remain: faith, hope, and love. But the greatest of these is love. The climactic statement of the chapter and arguably of the entire letter. Faith, hope, and love are the permanent realities -- they outlast every spiritual gift. And among even these three, love is supreme. Why? Because faith will become sight, hope will become fulfillment, but love will never be superseded. Love is the character of God himself, and it endures into eternity unchanged.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthians ranked themselves by spiritual gifts. Paul replaces their hierarchy with a completely different set of values: faith, hope, and love, with love at the top. This inverts the Corinthian value system entirely.
thenVsNow.now: The triad of faith, hope, and love has become foundational to Christian theology and ethics. Churches, charities, and individuals structure their identity around these three virtues. Paul's ranking -- love above even faith -- is a perpetual challenge to communities that emphasize correct belief over compassionate action.

explainer.summary: Paul applies the love principle of chapter 13 to the specific problem of worship in Corinth: the chaotic, competitive use of spiritual gifts, especially tongues. His argument is straightforward -- prophecy is superior to tongues in public worship because prophecy communicates intelligible content that builds up the community, while uninterpreted tongues edify only the speaker. Paul does not forbid tongues but insists they must be interpreted. He gives practical regulations: no more than two or three tongues-speakers per service, always with interpretation; prophets speak in turn and are subject to evaluation by others. Paul's goal is worship that is both Spirit-led and orderly, both passionate and intelligible.
themes: Prophecy over tongues in public worship
Intelligibility as the standard for worship
Order and decorum in the assembly
Building up the community as the purpose of gifts
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 14 is the practical application of chapters 12-13. Chapter 12 established that gifts are diverse and distributed by one Spirit. Chapter 13 established that love must govern all gifts. Now chapter 14 works out the implications: if love seeks the other's good, and if the purpose of gifts is the community's edification, then intelligible communication (prophecy) must take precedence over unintelligible ecstasy (tongues).

wordStudies.translatedAs: Speaking forth a message from God
wordStudies.meaning: Speaking forth a message from God. In Paul's usage, prophecy is not primarily predicting the future but delivering a divinely-inspired message that strengthens, encourages, and comforts the community (14:3). It is intelligible speech directed at the congregation's needs. Paul considers it the most valuable gift for public worship precisely because it communicates content.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Languages, whether human or ecstatic
wordStudies.meaning: Languages, whether human or ecstatic. In 1 Corinthians, tongues (glossolalia) appear to be ecstatic utterances not in any recognized human language -- speech directed to God in a spiritual language that requires interpretation to be understood by others. Paul values tongues for private devotion but insists they must be interpreted in public worship.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To build, construct, edify
wordStudies.meaning: To build, construct, edify. From oikos (house) and domeo (to build). Paul uses this construction metaphor repeatedly: worship practices should build up the community like a house being constructed. It is his primary criterion for evaluating any worship practice.

verses.text: Follow after love, and earnestly desire spiritual gifts, but especially that you may prophesy.
verses.explainer: Pursue love, and eagerly desire spiritual gifts, especially prophecy. The first word connects to chapter 13: pursue love. Then, within that framework of love, desire the gifts that benefit the community most -- especially prophecy.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthians were eagerly pursuing tongues as the mark of spiritual superiority. Paul redirects their zeal: if you want to be ambitious about gifts, be ambitious about prophecy, which actually helps other people.
thenVsNow.now: This verse establishes a principle for evaluating any worship practice: does it build up others? A church focused on spectacular experiences rather than intelligible edification is repeating the Corinthian error.

verses.text: For he who speaks in another language speaks not to men, but to God; for no one understands; but in the Spirit he speaks mysteries.
verses.explainer: For anyone who speaks in a tongue does not speak to people but to God. Indeed, no one understands them; they utter mysteries by the Spirit. Paul acknowledges the genuine spiritual nature of tongues -- the speaker is communicating with God, and the content is Spirit-given. But the key problem is that no one else understands.

verses.text: But he who prophesies speaks to men for their edification, exhortation, and consolation.
verses.explainer: But the one who prophesies speaks to people for their strengthening, encouraging and comfort. Prophecy is defined by its effect: it strengthens (builds up structurally), encourages (calls alongside to help), and comforts (brings consolation). These are relational, community-building outcomes.

verses.text: He who speaks in another language edifies himself, but he who prophesies edifies the assembly.
verses.explainer: Anyone who speaks in a tongue edifies themselves, but the one who prophesies edifies the church. The contrast is sharp: tongues build up the individual speaker; prophecy builds up the entire community. In Paul's framework, where love 'does not seek its own' (13:5), the communal benefit of prophecy clearly wins.

thenVsNow.then: Private edification was not a bad thing in Paul's view -- he spoke in tongues more than all of them (v.18). But in the assembly, where the purpose is mutual edification, self-focused gifts miss the point.
thenVsNow.now: This verse challenges worship practices designed primarily for the individual's experience rather than the community's growth. Any practice that says 'this is just between me and God' while ignoring the people sitting around you is tongues without interpretation.

verses.text: Now I desire to have you all speak with other languages, but rather that you would prophesy. For he is greater who prophesies than he who speaks with other languages, unless he interprets, that the assembly may be built up.
verses.explainer: Paul would like every one of them to speak in tongues, but he would rather they prophesy. The one who prophesies is greater than the one who speaks in tongues, unless someone interprets, so that the church may be edified. Paul is not anti-tongues. He simply insists on the priority of intelligibility in public worship. Tongues with interpretation achieves the same purpose as prophecy.

verses.text: But now, brothers, if I come to you speaking with other languages, what would I profit you, unless I speak to you either by way of revelation, or of knowledge, or of prophesying, or of teaching?
verses.explainer: Now, brothers and sisters, if I come to you speaking in tongues, what good will I be to you, unless I bring some revelation, knowledge, prophecy, or teaching? Paul uses himself as an example. Even an apostle speaking in tongues would be useless to the congregation without intelligible content.

verses.text: Even things without life, giving a voice, whether pipe or harp, if they didn’t give a distinction in the sounds, how would it be known what is piped or harped?
verses.explainer: Even lifeless instruments that produce sound -- a pipe or a harp -- if they do not give distinct notes, how will anyone know what tune is being played? The first of several analogies: musical instruments must play recognizable melodies to communicate anything.

verses.text: For if the trumpet gave an uncertain sound, who would prepare himself for war?
verses.explainer: If the trumpet does not sound a clear call, who will get ready for battle? A military analogy: the trumpet call must be distinct enough to convey a specific command. An unclear trumpet is worse than silence because it creates confusion.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Military trumpet calls', 'explanation': 'Roman military trumpets (tubae) had specific calls for advance, retreat, formation changes, and alarm. Soldiers trained to respond instantly to each distinct signal. An ambiguous trumpet sound could mean the difference between victory and disaster. Corinth, as a Roman colony, would have been familiar with military procedures.'}

verses.text: So also you, unless you uttered by the tongue words easy to understand, how would it be known what is spoken? For you would be speaking into the air.
verses.explainer: So it is with you. Unless you speak intelligible words with your tongue, how will anyone know what you are saying? You will just be speaking into the air. Paul applies the analogy directly: unintelligible speech in worship is like a garbled trumpet -- it communicates nothing and helps no one.

verses.text: There are, it may be, so many kinds of sounds in the world, and none of them is without meaning.
verses.explainer: Undoubtedly there are all sorts of languages in the world, and none of them is without meaning. Every language in the world communicates meaning to those who understand it. The problem is not that tongues lack content but that the content is inaccessible without interpretation.

verses.text: If then I don’t know the meaning of the sound, I would be to him who speaks a foreigner, and he who speaks would be a foreigner to me.
verses.explainer: If I do not grasp the meaning of what someone is saying, I am a foreigner to the speaker, and the speaker is a foreigner to me. The word for 'foreigner' is barbaros -- one whose language sounds like 'bar-bar-bar' to your ears. Without interpretation, the tongues-speaker and the congregation are mutual strangers.

verses.text: So also you, since you are zealous for spiritual gifts, seek that you may abound to the building up of the assembly.
verses.explainer: So it is with you. Since you are eager for gifts of the Spirit, try to excel in those that build up the church. Paul acknowledges their zeal and redirects it: be ambitious, but let building up the community be your measure of success.

verses.text: Therefore let him who speaks in another language pray that he may interpret.
verses.explainer: Therefore, anyone who speaks in a tongue should pray that they may interpret what they say. Practical guidance: if you have the gift of tongues, pray also for the gift of interpretation, so the community can benefit.

verses.text: For if I pray in another language, my spirit prays, but my understanding is unfruitful.
verses.explainer: For if I pray in a tongue, my spirit prays, but my mind is unfruitful. Paul distinguishes between the spirit (the dimension of the person engaged in tongues) and the mind (the rational, communicative faculty). In tongues, the spirit is active but the mind contributes nothing.

verses.text: What is it then? I will pray with the spirit, and I will pray with the understanding also. I will sing with the spirit, and I will sing with the understanding also.
verses.explainer: So what shall I do? I will pray with my spirit, but I will also pray with my understanding. I will sing with my spirit, but I will also sing with my understanding. Paul's solution is both/and: engage the spirit and the mind. Worship should be spiritually passionate and intellectually intelligible.

verses.text: Otherwise if you bless with the spirit, how will he who fills the place of the unlearned say the “Amen” at your giving of thanks, seeing he doesn’t know what you say?
verses.explainer: Otherwise, when you give thanks with your spirit, how can someone who finds themselves among the uninstructed say 'Amen' to your thanksgiving, since they do not know what you are saying? The 'Amen' was spoken congregationally to affirm a prayer or statement. If no one understands your prayer, no one can agree with it. The congregation is excluded from participation.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'The congregational Amen', 'explanation': "In both Jewish synagogue worship and early Christian gatherings, the congregation responded 'Amen' (Hebrew for 'so be it' or 'truly') to affirm prayers, blessings, and teachings. This was not passive listening but active participation. Unintelligible prayer robs the congregation of their voice."}

verses.text: For you most certainly give thanks well, but the other person is not built up.
verses.explainer: You are giving thanks well enough, but the other person is not edified. Paul concedes the quality of the tongues-speaker's prayer -- it may be genuine and beautiful. But the criterion is not the quality of the prayer but whether it builds up others.

verses.text: I thank my God, I speak with other languages more than you all.
verses.explainer: I thank God that I speak in tongues more than all of you. Paul drops a remarkable personal note: he has the gift of tongues to a greater degree than any of them. This is not false modesty; it preempts the accusation that he is against tongues because he does not have the gift.

verses.text: However in the assembly I would rather speak five words with my understanding, that I might instruct others also, than ten thousand words in another language.
verses.explainer: But in the church I would rather speak five intelligible words to instruct others than ten thousand words in a tongue. The ratio is staggering: five to ten thousand. Paul would take five comprehensible words over a ten-thousand-word ecstatic performance. Intelligibility trumps volume by a factor of two thousand to one.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthians measured spiritual power by the intensity and duration of ecstatic speech. Paul inverts their scale: five clear words outweigh an ocean of unintelligible sound.
thenVsNow.now: This verse speaks directly to any worship culture that values emotional intensity over intellectual content, or performance over participation. Paul insists that worship must communicate.

verses.text: Brothers, don’t be children in thoughts, yet in malice be babies, but in thoughts be mature.
verses.explainer: Brothers and sisters, stop thinking like children. In regard to evil be infants, but in your thinking be adults. Paul bluntly tells them their fascination with tongues is childish. Mature believers value what builds up the community, not what impresses the audience.

verses.text: In the law it is written, “By men of strange languages and by the lips of strangers I will speak to this people. Not even thus will they hear me, says the Lord.”
verses.explainer: In the Law it is written: 'With other tongues and through the lips of foreigners I will speak to this people, but even then they will not listen to me,' says the Lord. Paul quotes Isaiah 28:11-12 to argue that unintelligible speech is a sign of judgment, not blessing.

verses.text: Therefore other languages are for a sign, not to those who believe, but to the unbelieving; but prophesying is for a sign, not to the unbelieving, but to those who believe.
verses.explainer: Tongues, then, are a sign not for believers but for unbelievers; prophecy, however, is not for unbelievers but for believers. A surprising and debated statement. Paul seems to mean that tongues function as a negative sign (confirming unbelievers in their unbelief) while prophecy functions as a positive sign (building up believers). The next verses clarify with practical scenarios.

verses.text: If therefore the whole assembly is assembled together and all speak with other languages, and unlearned or unbelieving people come in, won’t they say that you are crazy?
verses.explainer: So if the whole church comes together and everyone speaks in tongues, and outsiders or unbelievers come in, will they not say that you are out of your minds? The practical test: what happens when a non-Christian walks into your worship service? If everyone is speaking in tongues simultaneously, the visitor concludes you are insane.

thenVsNow.then: Ecstatic religious behavior was common in Corinthian pagan worship, especially in the Dionysian and Cybele cults. If Christian worship looked identical, outsiders would see no difference.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is a foundational text for making worship accessible to visitors and seekers. It asks: what would a newcomer experience if they walked into our service? Would they encounter God, or would they encounter unintelligible chaos?

verses.text: But if all prophesy, and someone unbelieving or unlearned comes in, he is reproved by all, and he is judged by all.
verses.explainer: But if an unbeliever or outsider enters while everyone is prophesying, they are convicted by all, they are called to account by all. The contrasting scenario: intelligible prophetic speech penetrates the visitor's heart and produces conviction. The Spirit works through comprehensible words.

verses.text: And thus the secrets of his heart are revealed. So he will fall down on his face and worship God, declaring that God is among you indeed.
verses.explainer: The secrets of their hearts are laid bare. So they will fall down and worship God, exclaiming 'God is really among you!' This is the goal of Spirit-filled worship: not the community's self-edification but a manifestation of God's presence so powerful that outsiders are drawn to worship. Intelligibility serves evangelism.

verses.text: What is it then, brothers? When you come together, each one of you has a psalm, has a teaching, has a revelation, has another language, has an interpretation. Let all things be done to build each other up.
verses.explainer: What then shall we say, brothers and sisters? When you come together, each of you has a hymn, or a word of instruction, a revelation, a tongue, or an interpretation. Everything must be done so that the church may be built up. Paul describes early Christian worship as participatory, not spectator-driven. Multiple members contribute different elements. The organizing principle remains: everything must build up the community.

verses.text: If any man speaks in another language, let it be two, or at the most three, and in turn; and let one interpret.
verses.explainer: If anyone speaks in a tongue, two -- or at the most three -- should speak, one at a time, and someone must interpret. Practical regulation: a maximum of three tongues-speakers per service, sequential not simultaneous, and interpretation is mandatory.

verses.text: But if there is no interpreter, let him keep silent in the assembly, and let him speak to himself, and to God.
verses.explainer: If there is no interpreter, the speaker should keep quiet in the church and speak to himself and to God. Without an interpreter available, the tongues-speaker must remain silent in the assembly. Private devotional tongues are fine; public uninterpreted tongues are not.

verses.text: Let the prophets speak, two or three, and let the others discern.
verses.explainer: Two or three prophets should speak, and the others should weigh carefully what is said. Prophecy, too, is regulated: two or three per service, and the congregation evaluates the content. Prophecy in Paul's church is not an unchallenged oracle but a message subject to communal discernment.

verses.text: But if a revelation is made to another sitting by, let the first keep silent.
verses.explainer: If a revelation comes to someone who is sitting down, the first speaker should stop. Prophecy is not a monologue. If God gives a new revelation to another person, the current speaker yields. The Spirit distributes insight across the community.

verses.text: For you all can prophesy one by one, that all may learn, and all may be exhorted.
verses.explainer: For you can all prophesy in turn so that everyone may be instructed and encouraged. Prophecy is available to all and should be exercised orderly, in sequence. The result: instruction and encouragement for the entire community.

verses.text: The spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets,
verses.explainer: The spirits of prophets are subject to the control of prophets. Genuine prophecy does not overwhelm the speaker's self-control. The Spirit does not hijack the prophet; the prophet can choose when and how to speak. This distinguishes Christian prophecy from pagan ecstasy.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Prophetic self-control versus ecstasy', 'explanation': 'In pagan oracular traditions like the Pythia at Delphi, the prophet entered an uncontrolled trance state and had no memory of the message afterward. Paul insists that the Holy Spirit does not override human agency. Christian prophets remain in control of themselves -- a critical distinction from the ecstatic religions the Corinthians knew from their pagan past.'}

verses.text: for God is not a God of confusion, but of peace, as in all the assemblies of the saints.
verses.explainer: For God is not a God of disorder but of peace -- as in all the congregations of the Lord's people. This is the theological foundation for orderly worship: God's character is peace, not chaos. Worship should reflect the character of the God being worshipped.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthian assembly was apparently chaotic -- everyone speaking at once, no interpretation, no evaluation. Paul insists this does not reflect God's nature.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is invoked in debates about worship style. It does not demand rigid formality, but it does insist that worship be orderly enough for everyone to participate meaningfully and for outsiders to encounter God rather than confusion.

verses.text: Let the wives be quiet in the assemblies, for it has not been permitted for them to be talking except in submission, as the law also says,
verses.explainer: Women should remain silent in the churches. They are not allowed to speak, but must be in submission, as the law says. This is one of the most debated verses in the New Testament. It appears to contradict 11:5, where Paul assumes women pray and prophesy in worship. Scholars have proposed several explanations: (1) Paul is quoting a Corinthian slogan he will then refute; (2) the 'silence' refers to evaluating prophecy, not all speech; (3) these verses are a later interpolation not written by Paul; (4) Paul is addressing a specific local disruption, not issuing a universal rule.

thenVsNow.then: In both Jewish synagogues and Greco-Roman assemblies, women's public speech was restricted. However, Paul himself names women as co-workers, deacons, and even an apostle (Junia, Romans 16:7). The tension between this verse and Paul's wider practice is real.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is central to debates about women's roles in the church. Some traditions use it to prohibit women from teaching or leading; others argue it addresses a specific situation in Corinth (perhaps disruptive questioning) and does not establish a universal principle. The interpretation one adopts has profound implications for church governance.

verses.text: if they desire to learn anything. “Let them ask their own husbands at home, for it is shameful for a wife to be talking in the assembly.”
verses.explainer: If they want to inquire about something, they should ask their own husbands at home; for it is disgraceful for a woman to speak in the church. The mention of 'husbands' suggests the issue may involve married women disrupting the service with questions -- perhaps calling across the room to their husbands in a culture where men and women may have sat separately. The word for 'disgraceful' (aischron) is the same word used for shameful cultural violations, suggesting a local propriety issue.

verses.text: What!? Was it from you that the word of God went out? Or did it come to you alone?
verses.explainer: Or did the word of God originate with you? Or are you the only people it has reached? Paul's sharp retort -- possibly aimed at those who think their local practice overrides the broader church's custom. Some scholars read this as Paul's rejection of the silencing rule he just quoted from the Corinthians' own letter.

verses.text: If any man thinks himself to be a prophet, or spiritual, let him recognize the things which I write to you, that they are the commandment of the Lord.
verses.explainer: If anyone thinks they are a prophet or otherwise gifted by the Spirit, let them acknowledge that what I am writing to you is the Lord's command. Paul pulls rank: these are not personal opinions but the Lord's instructions. Anyone who claims to be spiritual should recognize this.

verses.text: But if anyone is ignorant, let him be ignorant.
verses.explainer: But if anyone ignores this, they will themselves be ignored. Blunt: if you refuse to accept these instructions, the community will disregard you. Spiritual authority carries the responsibility of submission to apostolic teaching.

verses.text: Therefore, brothers, desire earnestly to prophesy, and don’t forbid speaking with other languages.
verses.explainer: Therefore, my brothers and sisters, be eager to prophesy, and do not forbid speaking in tongues. Paul's summary is balanced: pursue prophecy enthusiastically, and do not ban tongues. Both are legitimate gifts of the Spirit.

verses.text: Let all things be done decently and in order.
verses.explainer: But everything should be done in a fitting and orderly way. The final word on worship: decency and order. Not deadness -- orderliness. The Spirit works through structure, not despite it.

explainer.summary: Chapter 15 is the theological climax of 1 Corinthians and the most sustained argument for the resurrection in the entire Bible. Some Corinthians were denying the future bodily resurrection of the dead -- possibly influenced by Greek philosophy, which despised the body and valued only the immortal soul. Paul responds with a massive, multi-layered argument: (1) the resurrection of Christ is the foundation of the gospel and is supported by eyewitness testimony including Paul's own; (2) if Christ is not raised, the entire Christian faith collapses; (3) Christ's resurrection is the 'firstfruits' -- the guarantee and prototype of the general resurrection; (4) the resurrection body will be radically transformed, not merely resuscitated; (5) at Christ's return, death itself will be destroyed. The chapter moves from historical evidence to theological argument to cosmic vision to pastoral exhortation.
themes: Christ's resurrection as historical fact and theological foundation
The logic of resurrection -- if Christ is raised then the dead are raised
The resurrection body -- continuity and transformation
Death as the last enemy to be destroyed
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 15 addresses the last major doctrinal problem in the letter. After dealing with factions (1-4), moral failures (5-6), marriage and food (7-10), worship abuses (11-14), Paul tackles the most fundamental question: is there a resurrection of the dead? If the answer is no, everything Paul has said collapses. This chapter is the theological foundation on which the entire letter rests.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The first portion of the harvest, offered to God a
wordStudies.meaning: The first portion of the harvest, offered to God as a pledge and guarantee that the full harvest will follow. In the Jewish agricultural calendar, the firstfruits offering (Leviticus 23:10) was brought at the beginning of harvest season. Paul calls Christ the 'firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' -- his resurrection is the first installment of the general resurrection, guaranteeing that the rest will follow.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A body animated and transformed by the Spirit (pne
wordStudies.meaning: A body animated and transformed by the Spirit (pneuma). This does not mean a body made of spirit (immaterial) but a physical body fully energized and governed by the Holy Spirit. Paul contrasts it with the soma psychikon (natural/soulish body) -- the present body animated by the psyche (soul/natural life). The resurrection body is not less physical than the present body but more so: it is the body perfected, no longer subject to decay, weakness, or death.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A truth previously hidden but now revealed by God
wordStudies.meaning: A truth previously hidden but now revealed by God. In Paul's usage, a mystery is not something unknowable but something that required divine disclosure. The 'mystery' of 15:51 is that not all believers will die before Christ's return -- some will be transformed instantly, 'in the twinkling of an eye.'

wordStudies.translatedAs: Victory, conquest, triumph
wordStudies.meaning: Victory, conquest, triumph. Paul uses it in his climactic declaration: 'Death is swallowed up in victory.' The word carries the connotation of a decisive military triumph -- death is not merely defeated but utterly consumed.

verses.text: Now I declare to you, brothers, the Good News which I preached to you, which also you received, in which you also stand,
verses.explainer: Now, brothers and sisters, I want to remind you of the gospel I preached to you, which you received and on which you have taken your stand. Paul opens by grounding the discussion in the gospel they already accepted. He is not introducing new doctrine but drawing out the implications of what they already believe.

verses.text: by which also you are saved, if you hold firmly the word which I preached to you—unless you believed in vain.
verses.explainer: By this gospel you are saved, if you hold firmly to the word I preached to you. Otherwise, you have believed in vain. The conditional 'if' is important: the gospel saves only those who continue to hold it. Denying the resurrection is not a minor disagreement -- it threatens the foundation of their faith.

verses.text: For I delivered to you first of all that which I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures,
verses.explainer: For what I received I passed on to you as of first importance: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures. This is the earliest written summary of the Christian gospel, predating all four Gospels. 'Received' and 'passed on' are technical terms for transmitting authoritative tradition. Paul did not invent this message; he inherited it from the earliest Christian community.

verses.text: that he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures,
verses.explainer: That he was buried, that he was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures. Four verbs form the gospel core: died, was buried, was raised, appeared. The burial confirms the reality of the death; the appearances confirm the reality of the resurrection. 'Was raised' is a perfect passive -- raised by God and remaining in the raised state.

verses.text: and that he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve.
verses.explainer: And that he appeared to Cephas, and then to the Twelve. Paul lists the resurrection witnesses, beginning with Peter (Cephas) as the first apostolic witness. 'The Twelve' is used as a title for the apostolic group even though Judas was no longer among them.

verses.text: Then he appeared to over five hundred brothers at once, most of whom remain until now, but some have also fallen asleep.
verses.explainer: After that, he appeared to more than five hundred of the brothers and sisters at the same time, most of whom are still living, though some have fallen asleep. This is a remarkable claim: a mass appearance to over five hundred people, most of whom are still alive when Paul writes. Paul is effectively saying: if you doubt me, go ask them. This is an invitation to verify the claim.

thenVsNow.then: Paul is writing within twenty-five years of the resurrection. Hundreds of eyewitnesses are still alive and can be interviewed. This is not a legend that developed over centuries but a claim made while the evidence could still be checked.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is a cornerstone of historical arguments for the resurrection. The appeal to living witnesses, made in a public letter that could be challenged, demonstrates remarkable confidence in the verifiability of the claim.

verses.text: Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles,
verses.explainer: Then he appeared to James, then to all the apostles. James is Jesus' brother, who was not a follower during Jesus' ministry (John 7:5) but became the leader of the Jerusalem church after the resurrection. His conversion from skeptic to leader is itself evidence for the resurrection.

verses.text: and last of all, as to the child born at the wrong time, he appeared to me also.
verses.explainer: And last of all he appeared to me also, as to one abnormally born. Paul includes himself in the witness list. His encounter with the risen Christ on the Damascus road (Acts 9) came after the ascension and was therefore abnormal in timing -- like a premature or untimely birth.

verses.text: For I am the least of the apostles, who is not worthy to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the assembly of God.
verses.explainer: For I am the least of the apostles and do not even deserve to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God. Paul's humility is genuine: he hunted Christians before becoming one. His past as a persecutor of the church is a permanent mark of unworthiness that only grace can overcome.

verses.text: But by the grace of God I am what I am. His grace which was given to me was not futile, but I worked more than all of them; yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me.
verses.explainer: But by the grace of God I am what I am, and his grace to me was not without effect. No, I worked harder than all of them -- yet not I, but the grace of God that was with me. One of Paul's most personal statements. Grace did not make Paul passive; it made him the hardest-working apostle. Yet he immediately corrects himself: not I, but grace. The tension between human effort and divine grace is held together, not resolved.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's transformation from persecutor to apostle was the most dramatic conversion story in the early church. His willingness to acknowledge his violent past while celebrating grace was a powerful testimony.
thenVsNow.now: This verse captures the Christian understanding of grace: it does not excuse inaction but empowers extraordinary effort. Paul works harder precisely because he knows he does not deserve to be there.

verses.text: Whether then it is I or they, so we preach, and so you believed.
verses.explainer: Whether, then, it is I or they, this is what we preach, and this is what you believed. Paul returns to the main point: the gospel of Christ's death and resurrection is the unanimous testimony of every apostle. This is not Paul's personal teaching; it is the universal Christian message.

verses.text: Now if Christ is preached, that he has been raised from the dead, how do some among you say that there is no resurrection of the dead?
verses.explainer: But if it is preached that Christ has been raised from the dead, how can some of you say that there is no resurrection of the dead? Now Paul pivots to the argument. Some Corinthians accept Christ's resurrection but deny the general resurrection of the dead. Paul will show that these two beliefs are logically inseparable.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Greek denial of bodily resurrection', 'explanation': 'Greek philosophy, especially Platonism, viewed the body as a prison for the immortal soul. Death liberated the soul from its bodily cage. The idea of a resurrected body was absurd to Greeks -- why would you want your prison back? This philosophical background explains why some Corinthians accepted spiritual immortality but rejected bodily resurrection.'}

verses.text: But if there is no resurrection of the dead, neither has Christ been raised.
verses.explainer: If there is no resurrection of the dead, then not even Christ has been raised. The logic is airtight: denying the possibility of resurrection in principle means denying Christ's resurrection as a specific instance. You cannot have one without the other.

verses.text: If Christ has not been raised, then our preaching is in vain, and your faith also is in vain.
verses.explainer: And if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is useless and so is your faith. The consequences of denying the resurrection begin to cascade. Without the resurrection, the gospel message is empty and faith is pointless.

verses.text: Yes, we are also found false witnesses of God, because we testified about God that he raised up Christ, whom he didn’t raise up, if it is so that the dead are not raised.
verses.explainer: More than that, we are then found to be false witnesses about God, for we have testified about God that he raised Christ from the dead. If Christ was not raised, the apostles are not merely mistaken -- they are liars who deliberately misrepresented God.

verses.text: For if the dead aren’t raised, neither has Christ been raised.
verses.explainer: For if the dead are not raised, then Christ has not been raised either. Paul restates the logical connection for emphasis: the general principle (the dead are raised) and the specific instance (Christ is raised) stand or fall together.

verses.text: If Christ has not been raised, your faith is vain; you are still in your sins.
verses.explainer: And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile; you are still in your sins. Without the resurrection, the cross accomplished nothing. Christ's death without his resurrection is just another execution. Sin remains unforgiven.

thenVsNow.then: For a community that had staked everything on the gospel -- leaving pagan temples, changing their moral behavior, enduring social ostracism -- the idea that their faith might be 'futile' was devastating.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's argument makes the resurrection non-negotiable for Christianity. Unlike other religions that could survive without a specific historical event, Christianity stands or falls with the bodily resurrection of Jesus. Remove it and the entire structure collapses.

verses.text: Then they also who are fallen asleep in Christ have perished.
verses.explainer: Then those also who have fallen asleep in Christ are lost. If there is no resurrection, believers who have already died are simply gone -- destroyed, not sleeping. The euphemism 'fallen asleep' implies they will wake up. Without resurrection, they will not.

verses.text: If we have only hoped in Christ in this life, we are of all men most pitiable.
verses.explainer: If only for this life we have hope in Christ, we are of all people most to be pitied. Christians who have endured persecution, poverty, and social exclusion for a gospel that turns out to be false are not noble -- they are pitiable fools.

verses.text: But now Christ has been raised from the dead. He became the first fruits of those who are asleep.
verses.explainer: But Christ has indeed been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep. The great 'but' that reverses the entire hypothetical. Christ is raised -- and not just for himself. He is the firstfruits, the first installment of the coming harvest. His resurrection guarantees ours.

verses.text: For since death came by man, the resurrection of the dead also came by man.
verses.explainer: For since death came through a man, the resurrection of the dead comes also through a man. Paul introduces the Adam-Christ parallel that will dominate the next section. One man introduced death; one man introduces resurrection. The symmetry is deliberate and cosmic.

verses.text: For as in Adam all die, so also in Christ all will be made alive.
verses.explainer: For as in Adam all die, so in Christ all shall be made alive. The two humanities: everyone in Adam (the entire human race) dies; everyone in Christ (those who belong to him) will be raised to life. The scope of 'all' in each clause is debated -- does 'all in Christ' mean every human being or only believers?

thenVsNow.then: The Adam-Christ typology was central to Paul's theology (see also Romans 5:12-21). It provided a framework for understanding how one person's action could have universal consequences.
thenVsNow.now: This verse fuels debates about universalism (will everyone eventually be saved?) and the scope of the atonement. Paul's primary point is the certainty and comprehensiveness of resurrection for those 'in Christ,' paralleling the certainty of death for those 'in Adam.'

verses.text: But each in his own order: Christ the first fruits, then those who are Christ’s, at his coming.
verses.explainer: But each in their own order: Christ the firstfruits, then at his coming, those who belong to him. Resurrection has a sequence: Christ first (already accomplished), then believers at his second coming. The word for 'order' (tagma) is a military term for troops arranged in ranks.

verses.text: Then the end comes, when he will deliver up the Kingdom to God, even the Father; when he will have abolished all rule and all authority and power.
verses.explainer: Then the end will come, when he hands over the kingdom to God the Father after he has destroyed all dominion, authority and power. After the resurrection, Christ will complete his conquest of every hostile power in the universe and then hand the completed kingdom to the Father. This is cosmic-scale theology.

verses.text: For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet.
verses.explainer: For he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet. Christ is reigning now, in the present age, and will continue until every enemy is subjugated. This is not a passive waiting period but an active reign.

verses.text: The last enemy that will be abolished is death.
verses.explainer: The last enemy to be destroyed is death. Death itself is personified as an enemy that Christ will ultimately annihilate. Not merely defeat -- destroy. Death is not a natural part of God's creation but an invader that will be expelled.

thenVsNow.then: In a world where death was omnipresent -- infant mortality, plague, violence, famine -- the promise that death itself would be destroyed was revolutionary. Death was the ultimate tyrant, and Paul declares it a defeated enemy.
thenVsNow.now: This verse grounds Christian hope in the face of mortality. Death is real and terrible, but it is not permanent. It is the last enemy, which means every other enemy has already been or will be defeated before it. And it, too, will fall.

verses.text: For, “He put all things in subjection under his feet.” But when he says, “All things are put in subjection”, it is evident that he is excepted who subjected all things to him.
verses.explainer: For he 'has put everything under his feet.' Now when it says that 'everything' has been put under him, it is clear that this does not include God himself, who put everything under Christ. Paul quotes Psalm 8:6 and carefully clarifies: Christ's sovereignty extends over everything except the Father who gave him that sovereignty.

verses.text: When all things have been subjected to him, then the Son will also himself be subjected to him who subjected all things to him, that God may be all in all.
verses.explainer: When he has done this, then the Son himself will be made subject to him who put everything under him, so that God may be all in all. The ultimate goal of all history: God being 'all in all.' Christ's reign is not an end in itself but the means by which God's complete sovereignty is restored over all creation. The Son's voluntary submission to the Father is the final act of the cosmic drama.

verses.text: Or else what will they do who are baptized for the dead? If the dead aren’t raised at all, why then are they baptized for the dead?
verses.explainer: Now if there is no resurrection, what will those do who are baptized for the dead? If the dead are not raised at all, why are people baptized for them? One of the most puzzling verses in the Bible. Some Corinthians were apparently being baptized on behalf of dead people -- a practice Paul neither endorses nor condemns but uses as evidence that even the Corinthians implicitly believe in resurrection.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Baptism for the dead', 'explanation': "The exact practice is unknown and heavily debated. Over forty interpretations have been proposed. The most straightforward reading is that some Corinthians were undergoing baptism vicariously on behalf of people who died before being baptized. Paul does not comment on the practice's validity; he merely points out that it presupposes resurrection. If the dead are not raised, the practice makes no sense."}

verses.text: Why do we also stand in jeopardy every hour?
verses.explainer: And as for us, why do we endanger ourselves every hour? Paul adds another practical argument: if there is no resurrection, the apostles' willingness to face constant danger is irrational.

verses.text: I affirm, by the boasting in you which I have in Christ Jesus our Lord, I die daily.
verses.explainer: I face death every day -- yes, just as surely as I boast about you in Christ Jesus our Lord. Paul's daily confrontation with mortality makes sense only if death is not the end.

verses.text: If I fought with animals at Ephesus for human purposes, what does it profit me? If the dead are not raised, then “let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die.”
verses.explainer: If I fought wild beasts in Ephesus with no more than human hopes, what have I gained? If the dead are not raised, 'Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die.' Paul may be speaking metaphorically of fierce opponents in Ephesus, or he may have literally faced wild animals in the arena. Either way, his point is that without resurrection, hedonism is the only rational philosophy.

verses.text: Don’t be deceived! “Evil companionships corrupt good morals.”
verses.explainer: Do not be misled: 'Bad company corrupts good character.' Paul quotes the Greek poet Menander (from his comedy Thais) to warn that associating with those who deny the resurrection will corrupt their moral lives. Theology shapes behavior: wrong beliefs produce wrong living.

verses.text: Wake up righteously, and don’t sin, for some have no knowledge of God. I say this to your shame.
verses.explainer: Come back to your senses as you ought, and stop sinning; for there are some who are ignorant of God -- I say this to your shame. Paul is blunt: denying the resurrection is not sophisticated theology but ignorance of God. And he says it to shame them.

verses.text: But someone will say, “How are the dead raised?” and, “With what kind of body do they come?”
verses.explainer: But someone will ask, 'How are the dead raised? With what kind of body will they come?' Paul anticipates the skeptic's question: the resurrection might be true in principle, but how does it actually work? What kind of body is a resurrection body?

verses.text: You foolish one, that which you yourself sow is not made alive unless it dies.
verses.explainer: How foolish! What you sow does not come to life unless it dies. Paul calls the question foolish and answers with an analogy from agriculture: a seed must die (be buried in the ground and decompose) before it can produce a new plant. Death is not the end of the body but the necessary transformation.

verses.text: That which you sow, you don’t sow the body that will be, but a bare grain, maybe of wheat, or of some other kind.
verses.explainer: When you sow, you do not plant the body that will be, but just a seed, perhaps of wheat or of something else. The seed analogy continues: what you plant looks nothing like what grows. An acorn does not look like an oak tree. The resurrection body will be continuous with the present body (same person) but radically different in form.

verses.text: But God gives it a body even as it pleased him, and to each seed a body of its own.
verses.explainer: But God gives it a body as he has determined, and to each kind of seed he gives its own body. God is the author of the transformation. Just as God determines what a wheat seed becomes (a wheat plant, not barley), God will determine the form of the resurrection body.

verses.text: All flesh is not the same flesh, but there is one flesh of men, another flesh of animals, another of fish, and another of birds.
verses.explainer: Not all flesh is the same: people have one kind of flesh, animals have another, birds another, and fish another. Paul expands the analogy: even in the present creation, God has made an enormous variety of bodies suited to different environments. The resurrection body is simply another variation in God's creative repertoire.

verses.text: There are also celestial bodies, and terrestrial bodies; but the glory of the celestial differs from that of the terrestrial.
verses.explainer: There are also heavenly bodies and there are earthly bodies; but the splendor of the heavenly bodies is one kind, and the splendor of the earthly bodies is another. The comparison extends to celestial bodies: sun, moon, and stars all have different kinds of glory. The resurrection body will have its own distinctive glory, suited to its heavenly environment.

verses.text: There is one glory of the sun, another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars; for one star differs from another star in glory.
verses.explainer: The sun has one kind of splendor, the moon another and the stars another; and star differs from star in splendor. Even among stars, there are differences in brightness and glory. Paul implies there may be diversity among resurrection bodies as well -- not uniformity but a shared, glorious transformation.

verses.text: So also is the resurrection of the dead. The body is sown perishable; it is raised imperishable.
verses.explainer: So will it be with the resurrection of the dead. The body that is sown is perishable, it is raised imperishable. Paul now applies the analogies with four contrasts. First: the present body decays and dies; the resurrection body cannot.

verses.text: It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power.
verses.explainer: It is sown in dishonor, it is raised in glory; it is sown in weakness, it is raised in power. Second and third contrasts: the present body is associated with the shame and frailty of death; the resurrection body is glorious and powerful. The word 'glory' (doxa) echoes the glory of the heavenly bodies in verse 40.

thenVsNow.then: In a culture where honor and shame were the primary social currencies, the promise that the body -- which in death is the ultimate object of shame -- would be raised in glory was profoundly hopeful.
thenVsNow.now: These contrasts speak to the universal human experience of bodily decline: aging, illness, disability, death. Paul promises not the abandonment of the body but its radical upgrade -- the same person in an imperishable, glorious, powerful body.

verses.text: It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body. There is a natural body and there is also a spiritual body.
verses.explainer: It is sown a natural body, it is raised a spiritual body. If there is a natural body, there is also a spiritual body. The fourth contrast is the most important and most misunderstood. 'Spiritual body' (soma pneumatikon) does not mean 'made of spirit' (immaterial) but 'animated and governed by the Spirit.' The present body is animated by the psyche (natural life); the resurrection body is animated by the pneuma (God's Spirit).

verses.text:
So also it is written, “The first man, Adam, became a living soul.”
 The last Adam became a life-giving spirit.
verses.explainer: So it is written: 'The first man Adam became a living being'; the last Adam, a life-giving spirit. Paul returns to the Adam-Christ contrast. Adam received life (a living being); Christ gives life (a life-giving spirit). The first Adam was animated; the last Adam animates.

verses.text: However that which is spiritual isn’t first, but that which is natural, then that which is spiritual.
verses.explainer: The spiritual did not come first, but the natural, and after that the spiritual. The order is important: natural first, spiritual second. Adam came before Christ; the perishable body comes before the imperishable one. History moves from lower to higher, from earthly to heavenly.

verses.text: The first man is of the earth, made of dust. The second man is the Lord from heaven.
verses.explainer: The first man was of the dust of the earth; the second man is of heaven. Adam's origin is earth (dust); Christ's origin is heaven. The resurrection body corresponds to Christ's heavenly nature, not Adam's earthly one.

verses.text: As is the one made of dust, such are those who are also made of dust; and as is the heavenly, such are they also that are heavenly.
verses.explainer: As was the earthly man, so are those who are of the earth; and as is the heavenly man, so also are those who are of heaven. The two Adams define two humanities. Those 'in Adam' bear the earthly image; those 'in Christ' will bear the heavenly image.

verses.text: As we have borne the image of those made of dust, let’s also bear the image of the heavenly.
verses.explainer: And just as we have borne the image of the earthly man, so shall we bear the image of the heavenly man. The promise: we will be transformed to bear Christ's image. The resurrection is not just survival after death but transformation into the likeness of the glorified Christ.

verses.text: Now I say this, brothers, that flesh and blood can’t inherit God’s Kingdom; neither does the perishable inherit imperishable.
verses.explainer: I declare to you, brothers and sisters, that flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God, nor does the perishable inherit the imperishable. This is not a denial of bodily resurrection but an insistence that the present form of the body must be transformed. Flesh and blood in its current perishable state cannot enter the eternal kingdom. Transformation is required.

verses.text: Behold, I tell you a mystery. We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed,
verses.explainer: Listen, I tell you a mystery: We will not all sleep, but we will all be changed. Paul reveals a 'mystery' -- a truth not previously disclosed: some believers will still be alive when Christ returns. They will not die first; they will be transformed directly from mortal to immortal.

thenVsNow.then: Paul apparently expected Christ's return within his own lifetime or soon after. The 'mystery' addresses a pastoral question: what happens to believers who are still alive at the second coming? They will be changed without passing through death.
thenVsNow.now: This verse shapes Christian eschatology and the doctrine of the 'rapture' (though Paul does not use that word here). The promise is that whether a believer has died or is still alive at Christ's return, all will be transformed.

verses.text: in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, and the dead will be raised incorruptible, and we will be changed.
verses.explainer: In a flash, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, the dead will be raised imperishable, and we will be changed. The transformation will be instantaneous. The 'last trumpet' echoes Jewish apocalyptic imagery where a trumpet signals God's final intervention in history.

verses.text: For this perishable body must become imperishable, and this mortal must put on immortality.
verses.explainer: For the perishable must clothe itself with the imperishable, and the mortal with immortality. The clothing metaphor: the resurrection is not stripping off the body but putting on a new one over it. The mortal body is not discarded but transformed -- clothed with immortality.

verses.text: But when this perishable body will have become imperishable, and this mortal will have put on immortality, then what is written will happen: “Death is swallowed up in victory.”
verses.explainer: When the perishable has been clothed with the imperishable, and the mortal with immortality, then the saying that is written will come true: 'Death has been swallowed up in victory.' Paul quotes Isaiah 25:8 to celebrate the final defeat of death. The verb 'swallowed up' (katepothee) is violent -- death is not gently retired but consumed, devoured, obliterated.

verses.text:
“Death, where is your sting?
Hades, where is your victory?”
verses.explainer: 'Where, O death, is your victory? Where, O death, is your sting?' Paul taunts death with a modified quotation from Hosea 13:14. Death, the universal tyrant, is mocked as a defeated enemy. Its victory is gone; its sting is removed.

verses.text: The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law.
verses.explainer: The sting of death is sin, and the power of sin is the law. A compact theological summary: death's weapon is sin (sin is what makes death destructive), and sin draws its power from the law (the law reveals sin and provokes it without providing the power to overcome it). Christ breaks this chain by dealing with sin and fulfilling the law.

verses.text: But thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.explainer: But thanks be to God! He gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. The doxology: the victory over death is a gift from God through Christ. It is not earned through human effort but received through faith in the risen Lord.

thenVsNow.then: For first-century believers living under Roman imperial power, where Caesar claimed to be lord and savior, declaring that victory comes 'through our Lord Jesus Christ' was a political as well as theological statement.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is among the most frequently quoted at Christian funerals. It transforms the occasion from a moment of despair to a declaration of victory. Death is real, but it is not final.

verses.text: Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the Lord’s work, because you know that your labor is not in vain in the Lord.
verses.explainer: Therefore, my dear brothers and sisters, stand firm. Let nothing move you. Always give yourselves fully to the work of the Lord, because you know that your labor in the Lord is not in vain. The practical conclusion of the entire resurrection argument. Because the resurrection is true, your work matters. Because death is defeated, your labor has eternal significance. This is not escapist theology but the most powerful motivation for engagement with the present world.

thenVsNow.then: The Corinthians might have wondered: if the resurrection is future, what difference does it make now? Paul's answer: it makes all the difference. Your present labor -- preaching, serving, suffering, loving -- is not pointless because the God who raises the dead will not waste any of it.
thenVsNow.now: This verse is the bridge between resurrection theology and daily life. It answers the question every believer asks: does what I do matter? Paul's answer is an emphatic yes -- because the resurrection guarantees that nothing done for the Lord is wasted.

explainer.summary: After the theological heights of chapter 15, Paul closes the letter with practical matters. He gives instructions for the collection for the Jerusalem church (a cause deeply important to him as a symbol of Gentile-Jewish unity), outlines his travel plans, commends Timothy and Apollos, offers final exhortations, greets various co-workers, and closes with a liturgical formula ('Maranatha -- come, Lord!') and a benediction. These mundane details reveal a living, breathing church with real logistics, relationships, and financial obligations.
themes: The collection for Jerusalem -- generosity and unity
