explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 16 is the typical closing section of a Pauline letter, combining practical instructions with personal notes and greetings. But even here, Paul's theology is visible: the collection embodies the unity he has argued for throughout the letter, and the closing 'Maranatha' connects the church's present practice to its hope in Christ's return.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A collection or gathering of money
wordStudies.meaning: A collection or gathering of money. This word appears only here in the New Testament. Paul organized a major collection from his Gentile churches for the impoverished Jewish believers in Jerusalem. The collection was both charitable and symbolic: it demonstrated the unity of Jewish and Gentile Christians and fulfilled Paul's promise to the Jerusalem apostles (Galatians 2:10).

wordStudies.translatedAs: An Aramaic phrase meaning either 'Our Lord, come!'
wordStudies.meaning: An Aramaic phrase meaning either 'Our Lord, come!' (a prayer for Christ's return) or 'Our Lord has come' (a confession of faith). The fact that Paul uses an Aramaic phrase in a letter to Greek-speaking Corinthians suggests it was a well-known liturgical formula in the early church, preserved in its original language like 'Amen' and 'Hallelujah.' It is one of the earliest Christian prayers on record.

verses.text: Now concerning the collection for the saints, as I commanded the assemblies of Galatia, you do likewise.
verses.explainer: Now about the collection for the Lord's people: Do what I told the Galatian churches to do. Another 'now about' formula responding to their letter. Paul has already given the same instructions to the churches in Galatia. The collection is a coordinated, multi-church effort.

verses.text: On the first day of the week, let each one of you save, as he may prosper, that no collections be made when I come.
verses.explainer: On the first day of every week, each one of you should set aside a sum of money in keeping with your income, saving it up, so that when I come no collections will have to be made. Paul's fundraising method is systematic: weekly, proportional to income, and accumulated over time so there is no last-minute scramble. The 'first day of the week' (Sunday) confirms that Christians were already gathering on the day of the resurrection.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'First day of the week', 'explanation': "This is one of the earliest references to Christians gathering on Sunday rather than the Jewish Sabbath (Saturday). Sunday was the day of Christ's resurrection, and it became the distinctive day for Christian assembly. Paul assumes this practice is already established in Corinth."}

verses.text: When I arrive, I will send whoever you approve with letters to carry your gracious gift to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Then, when I arrive, I will give letters of introduction to the men you approve and send them with your gift to Jerusalem. Paul distances himself from handling the money directly. The Corinthians choose their own delegates, Paul provides credentials, and the gift is delivered by people the church trusts. This transparency protects against accusations of financial impropriety.

thenVsNow.then: Paul had already been accused by some Corinthians of having impure motives (see 2 Corinthians 12:16-18). His insistence on financial transparency and community-selected delegates shows acute awareness of the need for accountability.
thenVsNow.now: Paul's financial practices -- transparency, community oversight, delegate accountability -- remain a model for church financial management. The principle is clear: those who handle ministry funds should welcome scrutiny.

verses.text: If it is appropriate for me to go also, they will go with me.
verses.explainer: If it seems advisable for me to go also, they will accompany me. Paul leaves open the possibility of going to Jerusalem himself, depending on the size and significance of the collection.

verses.text: But I will come to you when I have passed through Macedonia, for I am passing through Macedonia.
verses.explainer: After I go through Macedonia, I will come to you -- for I will be going through Macedonia. Paul outlines his travel itinerary: Macedonia first (northern Greece), then Corinth.

verses.text: But with you it may be that I will stay, or even winter, that you may send me on my journey wherever I go.
verses.explainer: Perhaps I will stay with you for a while, or even spend the winter, so that you can help me on my journey, wherever I go. 'Help me on my journey' (propempo) was a technical term for providing travel supplies, financial support, and companions for the next leg of a missionary's journey.

verses.text: For I do not wish to see you now in passing, but I hope to stay a while with you, if the Lord permits.
verses.explainer: For I do not want to see you now and make only a passing visit; I hope to spend some time with you, if the Lord permits. Paul wants a substantial stay, not a quick stopover. The qualifier 'if the Lord permits' acknowledges that his plans are subject to God's direction.

verses.text: But I will stay at Ephesus until Pentecost,
verses.explainer: But I will stay on at Ephesus until Pentecost. This verse dates the letter: Paul is writing from Ephesus and plans to stay through Pentecost (late spring). He will then travel through Macedonia to Corinth, arriving in time to winter there.

verses.text: for a great and effective door has opened to me, and there are many adversaries.
verses.explainer: Because a great door for effective work has opened to me, and there are many who oppose me. Two reasons to stay in Ephesus: opportunity and opposition. Paul sees both as signs he should remain. Opposition does not deter him; it confirms that significant work is being done.

thenVsNow.then: Paul's ministry in Ephesus was both fruitful and dangerous. Acts 19 describes the dramatic impact of his preaching (mass book-burnings of magic texts) and the violent opposition it provoked (the silversmith riot).
thenVsNow.now: Paul's attitude toward opposition is instructive: he does not flee from it but sees it as part of the landscape where God is working. Effective ministry and fierce resistance often go together.

verses.text: Now if Timothy comes, see that he is with you without fear, for he does the work of the Lord, as I also do.
verses.explainer: When Timothy comes, see to it that he has nothing to fear while he is with you, for he is carrying on the work of the Lord, just as I am. Paul commends Timothy, his protege, and asks the Corinthians to treat him well. The request that Timothy 'have nothing to fear' suggests Paul worries the Corinthians might not respect his younger delegate.

verses.text: Therefore let no one despise him. But set him forward on his journey in peace, that he may come to me; for I expect him with the brothers.
verses.explainer: Send him on his way in peace so that he may return to me. I am expecting him along with the brothers. Paul wants Timothy back safely and asks the Corinthians to provide for his return journey.

verses.text: Now concerning Apollos, the brother, I strongly urged him to come to you with the brothers; and it was not at all his desire to come now; but he will come when he has an opportunity.
verses.explainer: Now about our brother Apollos: I strongly urged him to go to you with the brothers. He was quite unwilling to go now, but he will go when he has the opportunity. A revealing note: Paul wanted Apollos to visit Corinth, but Apollos declined -- for now. The relationship between Paul and Apollos is cordial. There is no rivalry between them, even though some Corinthians had pitted them against each other.

verses.text: Watch! Stand firm in the faith! Be courageous! Be strong!
verses.explainer: Be on your guard; stand firm in the faith; be courageous; be strong. Four rapid military-style commands. Paul shifts from practical logistics to spiritual exhortation with the urgency of a commanding officer.

verses.text: Let all that you do be done in love.
verses.explainer: Do everything in love. After the four martial commands, Paul adds the overriding principle: love. This single sentence encapsulates the message of chapter 13 and the entire letter. Whatever you do -- stand firm, be courageous, fight -- do it all in love.

verses.text: Now I beg you, brothers (you know the house of Stephanas, that it is the first fruits of Achaia, and that they have set themselves to serve the saints),
verses.explainer: You know that the household of Stephanas were the first converts in Achaia, and they have devoted themselves to the service of the Lord's people. I urge you, brothers and sisters, Paul commends the household of Stephanas as the first converts in the region and models of service.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Household of Stephanas', 'explanation': "Stephanas and his household were Paul's first converts in Achaia (the Roman province including Corinth). Paul had baptized them personally (1:16). In the house church system, wealthy households that hosted and served the congregation held informal authority. Paul affirms their leadership as service-based, not status-based."}

verses.text: that you also be in subjection to such, and to everyone who helps in the work and labors.
verses.explainer: To submit to such people and to everyone who joins in the work and labors at it. Submission is to those who actually serve, not to those who claim status. Paul defines church authority by function (labor and service), not by position or social rank.

verses.text: I rejoice at the coming of Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus; for that which was lacking on your part, they supplied.
verses.explainer: I was glad when Stephanas, Fortunatus and Achaicus arrived, because they have supplied what was lacking from you. These three men apparently delivered the Corinthians' letter to Paul and served as his source of information about the church's situation.

verses.text: For they refreshed my spirit and yours. Therefore acknowledge those who are like that.
verses.explainer: For they refreshed my spirit and yours also. Such men deserve recognition. The personal warmth here reveals the human side of apostolic ministry: Paul is refreshed and encouraged by the presence of faithful friends.

verses.text: The assemblies of Asia greet you. Aquila and Priscilla greet you much in the Lord, together with the assembly that is in their house.
verses.explainer: The churches in the province of Asia send you greetings. Aquila and Priscilla greet you warmly in the Lord, and so does the church that meets at their house. Aquila and Priscilla (Prisca) had been Paul's co-workers in Corinth (Acts 18:2) and now host a house church in Ephesus. The Corinthians would have known them well.

verses.text: All the brothers greet you. Greet one another with a holy kiss.
verses.explainer: All the brothers and sisters here send you greetings. Greet one another with a holy kiss. The holy kiss was a common greeting in the early church, expressing the familial bond among believers. It was practiced between men and men, and women and women.

culturalNotes.title: Cultural Context
culturalNotes.note: {'term': 'Holy kiss', 'explanation': "A liturgical greeting practiced in early Christian worship (also mentioned in Romans 16:16, 2 Corinthians 13:12, 1 Thessalonians 5:26). It expressed familial love and unity among believers. By the second century, the kiss was formalized in the liturgy and gender-segregated. It persists today in some traditions as the 'kiss of peace' or 'passing the peace.'"}

verses.text: This greeting is by me, Paul, with my own hand.
verses.explainer: I, Paul, write this greeting in my own hand. Paul typically dictated his letters to a secretary (amanuensis) and then added a closing greeting in his own handwriting as authentication. The shift to his own hand signals the letter's conclusion and guarantees its authenticity.

verses.text:
If any man doesn’t love the Lord Jesus Christ, let him be accursed.
 Come, Lord!
verses.explainer: If anyone does not love the Lord, let that person be cursed! Come, Lord! (Maranatha). Two jarring elements in one verse: a curse formula and an Aramaic prayer. 'Maranatha' is one of the oldest Christian prayers preserved in its original Aramaic, suggesting it was so well-known that even Greek-speaking churches used it. It expresses the church's longing for Christ's return.

verses.text: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with you.
verses.explainer: The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you. The standard Pauline benediction: grace, the undeserved favor of God, mediated through the Lord Jesus Christ. After a letter full of rebuke, correction, and warning, Paul closes with grace.

verses.text: My love to all of you in Christ Jesus. Amen.
verses.explainer: My love to all of you in Christ Jesus. Amen. The final word is love -- the theme that has pervaded the entire letter. Paul has been stern, sarcastic, tender, and theologically soaring. He ends with the simplest possible statement of pastoral affection: I love you all in Christ Jesus.
